proposals.title: Systematic Evidence Synthesis for the 2025 Update of Clinical Practice Guidelines in Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD)
proposals.motivation: The field of MASLD (formerly NAFLD/NASH) is rapidly evolving, with new diagnostic tools, risk stratification algorithms, and therapeutic trial data emerging annually. As of 2025, clinical practice guidelines risk becoming outdated, potentially leading to heterogeneous and suboptimal patient care. A systematic, evidence-based update is critical to integrate the latest findings on non-invasive tests, fibrosis progression, and metabolic risk factors into actionable standards for clinicians.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic review and meta-analysis of evidence published in the preceding 24 months will identify significant new data that necessitates substantive updates to existing MASLD clinical practice guidelines, particularly regarding the use of non-invasive tests in specific patient subgroups (e.g., with type 2 diabetes or advanced obesity).
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic literature review (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Central) for studies published from January 2024 to December 2025. Using pre-defined PICO frameworks, we will extract data on diagnostic accuracy of non-invasive tests (e.g., FIB-4, ELF, VCTE), outcomes of pharmacologic trials (e.g., resmetirom, lanifibranor), and natural history studies. Data will be synthesized using meta-analytic techniques (random-effects models) where appropriate. The GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluations) framework will be applied to assess the quality of evidence and strength of recommendations for guideline integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, transparent evidence base for the next annual revision of major society guidelines (e.g., AASLD, EASL). It will directly inform updates on risk stratification, monitoring intervals, and treatment pathways for MASLD patients. The output will be a co-published evidence report and a summary of proposed guideline modifications.
proposals.novelty: While annual reviews exist, this proposal focuses specifically on the high-impact transition period where new pharmacotherapies and refined diagnostic strategies are becoming available, requiring a focused synthesis to bridge emerging trial data with immediate clinical practice needs. It applies rigorous systematic review methodology explicitly for the purpose of guideline updating, a structured process that is not yet standardized in hepatology.

proposals.title: Disentangling Resource Scarcity from Clinical Ambiguity: A Mixed-Methods Analysis of MASLD Management Gaps in Young Adults (18-35) in the Middle East and North Africa
proposals.motivation: MASLD prevalence remains high in MENA populations, yet young adults (18-35) are understudied, often presenting with significant fibrosis despite lower rates of traditional metabolic drivers like type 2 diabetes. This creates a critical public health blind spot. Current management debates focus on guideline clarity, but in resource-constrained settings, the primary barrier to effective care may be systemic limitations rather than clinical confusion.
proposals.hypothesis: In young MENA adults with MASLD, systemic resource constraints (e.g., limited access to non-invasive fibrosis tests, hepatology specialists, and structured follow-up) are a stronger driver of suboptimal management and poor outcomes than a lack of clear clinical guidelines or provider knowledge.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multinational, multi-center study across 3-5 MENA countries. First, a validated survey will quantify perceived management barriers among 200+ primary care and gastroenterology providers. Second, we will perform a retrospective cohort analysis of 500+ MASLD patients aged 18-35, mapping their actual care pathways (diagnostic tests, referrals, follow-up intervals) against guideline-recommended care and linking these to longitudinal outcomes (fibrosis progression, decompensation). Statistical analysis will use multivariable logistic regression to identify independent predictors of guideline-discordant care, with a primary focus on institutional resource variables versus provider-related factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence ranking the relative importance of resource vs. knowledge barriers in MASLD care for a young, at-risk MENA population. It will generate a region-specific, actionable framework to guide health policy, showing whether interventions should prioritize resource allocation (e.g., diagnostic infrastructure) or provider education. It will also characterize the clinical phenotype and natural history of MASLD in this understudied demographic.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond documenting guideline confusion or prevalence, instead directly testing the untested assumption that resource scarcity is the dominant barrier in young adults within constrained health systems, a population conspicuously absent from most MASLD management trials and cohort studies.

proposals.title: Impact of the Global MASLD/MASH Consensus on Clinical Practice Patterns and Intermediate Patient Outcomes: A Prospective, Multi-Center Implementation Study
proposals.motivation: The 2023-2024 global consensus statements on MASLD/MASH diagnosis and management represent a critical step towards standardized care. However, the real-world implementation and effectiveness of these recommendations in diverse clinical settings remain unknown. Assessing their adoption and impact on key processes of care (e.g., risk stratification, appropriate use of non-invasive tests, and referral pathways) is essential to translate expert guidance into improved patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic implementation of the global consensus recommendations in a structured clinical pathway will lead to a significant increase in guideline-concordant care and an improvement in intermediate patient outcomes, including more accurate identification of advanced fibrosis and timely intervention, compared to pre-implementation historical controls.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center implementation study across 10-15 hepatology clinics. A pre-post design will be used, comparing a 12-month pre-implementation period (historical control) to a 12-month post-implementation period. The intervention is a structured clinical pathway embedding consensus recommendations, including the use of the MASLD Clinical Care Pathway (CCP) algorithm for risk stratification, mandated FIB-4/ELFT calculation for all patients, and defined referral triggers for hepatology and advanced imaging (VCTE/MRE). Primary outcomes will be rates of guideline-concordant care. Secondary outcomes will include changes in the proportion of patients correctly stratified for advanced fibrosis and time to appropriate specialist evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the feasibility and effectiveness of implementing the global MASLD/MASH consensus. It will identify key barriers and facilitators to adoption in clinical practice. The findings will directly inform health systems and payers on strategies to optimize care delivery for this large patient population.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has validated individual diagnostic tests, this study uniquely evaluates the integrated implementation of the entire consensus-based care pathway and its effect on clinical decision-making, moving beyond diagnostic accuracy to assess practice change.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Clinical Guidelines for Metabolic Syndrome and Associated Cardiometabolic Diseases: A Systematic Delphi Consensus Approach
proposals.motivation: The management of metabolic syndrome and its associated diseases (e.g., type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, obesity) is hampered by fragmented and often conflicting clinical guidelines from different professional societies. This inconsistency creates confusion for clinicians, leads to variable patient care, and complicates the design of multinational clinical trials and public health strategies. A standardized, consensus-driven framework is urgently needed to unify core management principles across these interconnected conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a formalized document search and modified Delphi consensus methodology, previously validated in MASLD, will successfully identify and harmonize core, actionable clinical recommendations across international guidelines for metabolic syndrome and its key cardiometabolic sequelae.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a systematic search for published clinical practice guidelines (2015-2025) from major hepatology, endocrinology, cardiology, and obesity societies globally. Using natural language processing (NLP) tools (e.g., topic modeling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation) and manual extraction, we will map areas of alignment and discordance in diagnostic criteria, treatment targets, and monitoring strategies. A multi-disciplinary panel of international experts will then participate in a three-round, web-based modified Delphi process to vote on and refine a core set of harmonized statements, with consensus defined a priori as ≥80% agreement.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first internationally harmonized, multi-society consensus document on the integrated management of metabolic syndrome and its cardiometabolic complications. It will provide a clear, evidence-based roadmap for clinicians, inform health policy, and establish a reproducible methodological template for guideline harmonization in other chronic disease areas beyond hepatology.
proposals.novelty: While Delphi methods are established, their application to systematically reconcile guidelines across multiple, distinct but metabolically linked disease domains (endocrinology, cardiology, hepatology) is novel. This moves beyond single-disease consensus to create a holistic management framework reflective of real-world patient comorbidities.

proposals.title: Non-Linear Escalation of Cancer Risk in MASLD: Identifying Critical Thresholds of Steatosis and Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: The global burden of MASLD is immense, with a significant proportion of patients progressing to advanced fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). While cancer risk is known to increase with disease severity, the precise relationship remains poorly defined. Determining whether cancer risk escalates linearly or exhibits a sharp, non-linear increase at specific disease stages is critical for risk stratification, surveillance allocation, and clinical trial design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the risk of incident cancer, particularly HCC and extra-hepatic cancers, escalates in a non-linear, threshold-dependent manner with increasing severity of hepatic steatosis and fibrosis, rather than following a simple linear relationship.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational cohort study using harmonized data from existing MASLD registries and databases. Patients will be stratified by histologic (or non-invasive) grades of steatosis (SAF score) and stages of fibrosis (F0-F4). The primary outcome will be time-to-incident cancer (HCC and specific extra-hepatic cancers). We will employ segmented regression (piecewise linear) and restricted cubic spline models within a Cox proportional hazards framework to identify inflection points where the hazard ratio for cancer risk changes slope significantly. Sensitivity analyses will adjust for age, diabetes, obesity, and other metabolic risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative evidence for critical disease severity thresholds that trigger a disproportionate increase in cancer risk. It will directly inform evidence-based guidelines for initiating cancer surveillance in MASLD patients. The findings will also highlight the urgent need for aggressive therapeutic intervention before these risk thresholds are crossed.
proposals.novelty: While prior work associates advanced fibrosis with higher cancer risk, this study uniquely tests for non-linear inflection points, moving beyond linear hazard models. It integrates detailed histologic steatosis grading with fibrosis staging to identify compound thresholds, a nuanced approach not previously undertaken in large cohort studies.

proposals.title: Comparative Cancer Risk in NAFLD vs. MASLD Populations: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis of the NHANES III-Linked Mortality Data
proposals.motivation: The 2023 redefinition from NAFLD to MASLD explicitly incorporates cardiometabolic risk, potentially altering the identified at-risk population. Given the established link between metabolic dysfunction and cancer, it is critical to determine if this nosological shift changes the associated cancer risk profile, which has direct implications for patient risk stratification and screening guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: The population identified by the new MASLD criteria will have a similar overall and site-specific cancer risk compared to the population historically identified by NAFLD criteria, as the core metabolic drivers of carcinogenesis remain central to both definitions.
proposals.method: We will utilize the publicly available NHANES III (1988-1994) dataset with linked mortality data through 2019. Participants will be classified into NAFLD (by hepatic steatosis index [HSI] >36) and MASLD (by HSI >36 plus ≥1 cardiometabolic criterion) cohorts. We will perform competing risk survival analysis using Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard models to compare cancer-specific mortality (overall and for liver, colorectal, and other obesity-related cancers) between the cohorts, adjusting for age, sex, race, smoking, and diabetes duration. Sensitivity analyses will use the Fatty Liver Index (FLI) for steatosis definition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on whether the MASLD definition meaningfully alters cancer risk prediction. It will inform clinicians and guideline bodies on the continuity of cancer surveillance needs and validate the clinical utility of the new nomenclature from an oncological perspective.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared the epidemiological prevalence of NAFLD and MASLD, no study has yet investigated the longitudinal cancer outcomes associated with this diagnostic transition, making this a critical evidence gap in the post-2023 landscape.

proposals.title: Pharmacoepidemiologic Assessment of Metabolic Syndrome Therapies on Hepatocellular and Extrahepatic Cancer Incidence in Patients with MASLD: A Multi-Database Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Patients with MASLD, particularly those with advanced fibrosis, have a significantly elevated risk of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and emerging evidence suggests increased risk for certain extrahepatic cancers. While drugs for metabolic syndrome (e.g., GLP-1 RAs, SGLT2 inhibitors, pioglitazone) are used to treat MASLD components, their long-term impact on cancer incidence in this high-risk population remains poorly characterized in real-world settings. Determining if these commonly prescribed medications modulate cancer risk would inform clinical management and secondary prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the use of specific pharmacotherapies for metabolic syndrome (GLP-1 receptor agonists, SGLT2 inhibitors, and pioglitazone) is associated with a reduced incidence of hepatocellular carcinoma and specific extrahepatic cancers (colorectal, breast, pancreatic) in patients with MASLD, independent of baseline metabolic risk factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using large, linked healthcare databases (e.g., US claims/electronic health record data like Optum or TriNetX, and international counterparts). Patients with MASLD will be identified using validated algorithms based on ICD codes, exclusion of other liver diseases, and presence of metabolic risk factors. New users of target drug classes will be matched via high-dimensional propensity scores to non-users or users of alternative therapies. Primary outcomes will be incident HCC and extrahepatic cancers, identified via oncology registries or validated coding sequences. We will use Cox proportional hazards models to estimate adjusted hazard ratios, with sensitivity analyses to address confounding by indication and immortal time bias.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world evidence on the potential chemopreventive effects of metabolic drugs on cancer outcomes in MASLD, directly addressing a critical knowledge gap in patient management. It will generate risk estimates that can guide therapeutic selection and inform the design of future prospective trials for cancer prevention in this growing patient population.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links MASLD to cancer risk and metabolic drugs to liver histology, this study uniquely applies pharmacoepidemiologic methods to quantify the association between these specific drug exposures and hard oncologic endpoints across a broad MASLD population, moving beyond surrogate markers.

proposals.title: Disparities in Liver Transplant Evaluation and Listing for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) vs. Alcoholic Liver Disease (ALD): A Population-Based Analysis of Disease Burden and Access
proposals.motivation: Liver transplantation (LT) is a life-saving intervention for advanced liver disease. Pre-2026 data shows a rising prevalence of MASLD, yet its representation on LT waitlists may not reflect this burden, potentially due to referral biases, complex metabolic comorbidities, or stigma compared to ALD. Understanding these disparities is critical for equitable resource allocation and improving care pathways for the growing MASLD population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ratio of patients with MASLD listed for LT is significantly lower than the ratio of patients with ALD listed for LT, relative to their respective population-based prevalences of advanced liver disease, indicating a systematic disparity in the transplant evaluation pathway.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational cohort study linking population-level health administrative databases (e.g., US National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES) for prevalence, United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS) for listings). We will estimate the population prevalence of advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis due to MASLD and ALD using validated non-invasive tests (FIB-4, NFS) and diagnostic codes. We will calculate listing ratios (waitlist registrants / estimated prevalent advanced cases) for each etiology. Multivariable logistic regression will model the odds of listing, adjusting for age, sex, comorbidities (diabetes, obesity, cardiovascular disease), and socioeconomic factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative assessment of the gap between disease burden and transplant access for MASLD versus ALD. It will identify specific patient subgroups and systemic factors contributing to disparities, informing targeted interventions to improve referral networks and equitable LT evaluation. The findings will directly impact health policy and clinical practice guidelines for managing advanced MASLD.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described rising LT listings for MASLD, this study directly tests the assumption that listing reflects true burden by benchmarking against population-based prevalence data, a method not previously applied to compare MASLD and ALD. It moves beyond describing trends to quantifying a potential access inequity.

proposals.title: Geographic and Temporal Disparities in Liver Transplantation for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis and Alcoholic Liver Disease: A UNOS/OPTN Registry Analysis (2010-2025)
proposals.motivation: The rising prevalence of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) and the persistent burden of alcoholic liver disease (ALD) are driving a shift in liver transplant (LT) indications. However, the growth of MASH and ALD as LT indications is not uniform, with significant regional variations in disease prevalence, healthcare access, and transplant center practices. Understanding these geographic disparities is critical for equitable public health planning and resource allocation to address the impending epidemic of advanced liver disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the growth rates of MASH and ALD as primary indications for liver transplantation in the United States exhibit significant geographic clustering, which is independently associated with regional variations in socioeconomic factors, obesity and diabetes prevalence, and alcohol consumption patterns.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the United Network for Organ Sharing (UNOS)/Organ Procurement and Transplantation Network (OPTN) Standard Transplant Analysis and Research (STAR) files from 2010-2025. The primary outcome is the annual percentage change in LT listings for MASH and ALD, stratified by UNOS region and donor service area. We will employ spatial autocorrelation analysis (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify geographic clusters of high growth. Multivariable hierarchical Bayesian regression models will be used to assess the association between growth clusters and county-level covariates from CDC and US Census data, including obesity/diabetes rates, median income, insurance coverage, and alcohol sales.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed spatial epidemiology map of LT trends for MASH and ALD in the US, identifying specific regions at highest risk for a disproportionate future burden. It will quantify the contribution of modifiable metabolic and socioeconomic drivers to these disparities, offering actionable data for targeted public health interventions and informing national transplant policy to ensure equitable access.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have documented the national rise of MASH in transplant registries, this analysis uniquely applies spatial statistics and hierarchical modeling to uncover and explain subnational geographic disparities, moving beyond description to mechanistic, policy-relevant insights.

proposals.title: Development and Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital, Culturally-Tailored Education Program to Improve Disease Knowledge and Self-Management in Patients with Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD)
proposals.motivation: The global prevalence of MASLD is high, yet public and patient awareness remains critically low, often leading to late diagnosis and poor engagement with lifestyle interventions. This knowledge gap is exacerbated in high-risk subgroups, such as those with type 2 diabetes and obesity, as highlighted by Dr. Younossi's recent multinational diagnostic study. Effective, scalable education is urgently needed to empower patients, improve self-management, and potentially reduce the long-term economic burden of advanced liver disease.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, digital education program tailored to patient subgroups (e.g., by age, diabetes status) will significantly improve disease-specific knowledge, self-reported engagement in recommended lifestyle modifications, and health-related quality of life compared to standard-of-care informational handouts.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-module digital education platform (web/mobile app) using principles from the Health Belief Model and adult learning theory. Content will be culturally and linguistically adapted and include interactive elements (e.g., quizzes, goal-setting). In a 6-month, multi-center randomized controlled trial, 400 patients with MASLD (with/without significant fibrosis) will be assigned to the intervention or a control group receiving standard pamphlets. Primary outcomes are changes in validated MASLD knowledge and self-efficacy scores. Secondary outcomes include changes in physical activity (accelerometer data), dietary quality (validated questionnaires), and health-related quality of life (SF-36). Analysis will use intention-to-treat with mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the efficacy of a structured digital education program in MASLD. It will yield a scalable intervention toolkit and identify which patient subgroups benefit most, informing future public health strategies. The findings will directly address the critical gap between disease prevalence and patient understanding, a theme central to Dr. Younossi's work on MASLD as a public health challenge.
proposals.novelty: While patient education is common in diabetes and obesity, a rigorously tested, digitally-delivered, and subgroup-tailored program specifically for MASLD is novel. This moves beyond generic lifestyle advice to provide disease-specific education that addresses the unique misconceptions and prognostic concerns of MASLD patients.

proposals.title: Development and Pragmatic Assessment of a Multidisciplinary, Risk-Stratified Care Pathway for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) in a Real-World Cohort
proposals.motivation: The rising global prevalence of MASLD, particularly among patients with type 2 diabetes and obesity, presents a major public health challenge. Current care is fragmented, with poor coordination between primary care, hepatology, and metabolic specialists, leading to delayed diagnosis, inconsistent risk stratification, and suboptimal management of advanced fibrosis. This fragmentation contributes to the high economic burden of liver-related complications, as highlighted by Dr. Younossi's prior cost-of-illness studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a standardized, integrated care pathway, which connects primary care screening with specialist-led risk stratification and multidisciplinary management, will improve the timely identification of patients with advanced fibrosis, enhance guideline-concordant care, and reduce healthcare resource utilization compared to usual care.
proposals.method: We will design a risk-stratified pathway using pre-2026 non-invasive tests (NITs) like FIB-4 and vibration-controlled transient elastography (VCTE) as decision nodes. In a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial, primary care clinics will be assigned to implement the new pathway or continue usual care. The pathway algorithm will direct low-risk patients to primary care management, intermediate-risk for hepatology consultation, and high-risk for expedited multidisciplinary review (hepatology, endocrinology, dietetics). Primary outcomes will be the proportion of high-risk patients receiving appropriate specialist evaluation within 90 days and total MASLD-related healthcare costs over 18 months, analyzed using generalized linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, operational blueprint for health systems to manage the MASLD epidemic efficiently. It will generate critical evidence on the clinical and economic impact of integrated care, directly informing health policy. The study will also yield insights into real-world adherence to screening and referral protocols across diverse patient subgroups.
proposals.novelty: While multidisciplinary care is advocated, this proposal moves beyond conceptual frameworks to a pragmatic, algorithm-driven trial that embeds validated NITs into a scalable care pathway and measures hard endpoints like cost and care timeliness, which are understudied in MASLD management models.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Physical Activity and Sedentary Behavior to Predict Risk of Fibrosis Progression in Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD): A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The progression of MASLD to advanced fibrosis is a major driver of liver-related morbidity and mortality, yet risk stratification remains challenging. Current epidemiological models rely heavily on static clinical variables and self-reported lifestyle data, which are prone to recall bias and fail to capture real-world, dynamic behavioral patterns. A scalable, objective method to quantify modifiable risk behaviors is urgently needed for precision prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that digital phenotypes derived from passive smartphone sensor data—specifically quantifying patterns of physical activity, sedentariness, and circadian regularity—will be independently associated with the risk of fibrosis progression in patients with MASLD, providing superior predictive accuracy compared to traditional clinical models.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll 500 adults with biopsy-proven MASLD and baseline fibrosis stage F0-F2. Participants will install a custom research app that passively collects GPS, accelerometer, and screen-state data for 12 months. We will engineer features (e.g., daily step count variance, sedentary bout duration, location entropy) and use unsupervised learning (k-means clustering) to define behavioral phenotypes. The primary outcome is fibrosis progression (≥1-stage increase by FIB-4/ELF or biopsy). We will use Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for metabolic risk factors, to test associations between digital phenotypes and progression risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel, objective framework for quantifying real-world behavioral risk in MASLD, moving beyond self-report. It will generate the first validated digital biomarkers for fibrosis progression risk, enabling targeted behavioral interventions. The findings will directly inform the development of low-burden, continuous remote monitoring tools for high-risk patient cohorts.
proposals.novelty: While digital phenotyping has been explored in mental health and cardiology, its application to chronic liver disease, specifically for predicting hard histological outcomes like fibrosis progression, is novel. This approach moves beyond associating digital features with disease prevalence to actively modeling longitudinal risk of a key clinical endpoint.

proposals.title: Geographic Heterogeneity in Diagnostic Accuracy and Its Impact on Global MASLD Burden Estimates: A Systematic Analysis of Regional Data Sources and Clinical Practices
proposals.motivation: Global estimates of MASLD prevalence and mortality are derived from studies using varied diagnostic tools (e.g., ultrasound, FibroScan, algorithms) with differing accuracy. Pre-2026 evidence shows these tools perform differently across patient subgroups (e.g., obesity, T2D), but it is unknown how regional disparities in healthcare access, diagnostic protocols, and disease awareness systematically bias global epidemiological trends. This obscures the true disease burden and hampers equitable resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: Regional variations in the application and accuracy of non-invasive tests for MASLD and fibrosis significantly bias reported global prevalence and mortality trends, with under-diagnosis being most pronounced in regions with lower healthcare resource availability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a meta-epidemiological study. First, we will systematically map pre-2026 multinational cohort studies and national health surveys reporting MASLD prevalence, stratifying them by diagnostic modality (imaging, biomarkers, biopsy) and region (WHO regions). Second, using a Bayesian hierarchical model, we will adjust reported prevalence estimates by incorporating known test performance characteristics (sensitivity/specificity from prior diagnostic accuracy studies, including Dr. Younossi's work) and regional data on healthcare infrastructure (e.g., imaging device density). Third, we will model the potential impact of diagnostic misclassification on mortality attribution using Global Burden of Disease data inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of how diagnostic heterogeneity distorts global MASLD epidemiology. It will generate adjusted, region-specific prevalence estimates and identify geographic 'hotspots' of potential under-diagnosis. The findings will inform WHO and public health bodies on where to prioritize diagnostic capacity building and validate surveillance methodologies.
proposals.novelty: While diagnostic accuracy of individual tests is studied, no pre-2026 analysis has systematically modeled how the *differential application* of these tests across health systems creates a composite bias in global burden estimates, moving beyond patient-level to system-level diagnostic variability.

proposals.title: Incidence and Dynamic Risk Factors for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD): A Longitudinal Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on MASLD is dominated by cross-sectional studies reporting high prevalence, which conflates historical and current risk exposures. This static view limits our understanding of the evolving drivers of new disease onset, particularly in the context of rising obesity and type 2 diabetes (T2D) epidemics. Shifting focus to incidence is critical for identifying modifiable, contemporary risk factors to inform targeted primary prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the incidence rate of MASLD is accelerating disproportionately in specific demographic and metabolic subgroups, and that dynamic changes in weight, glycemic control, and novel serum biomarkers are stronger predictors of new-onset MASLD than baseline status alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal analysis of patients without baseline liver disease from large, multinational electronic health record databases (e.g., TriNetX, INSIGHT). Using a time-to-event (Cox proportional hazards) framework, we will calculate annualized incidence rates. The primary exposure variables will be time-varying covariates, including trajectories of BMI, HbA1c, and lipid profiles. We will employ machine learning algorithms, specifically gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), to model complex, non-linear interactions between these dynamic risk factors and new MASLD diagnosis, defined by validated non-invasive tests (e.g., FIB-4 < 1.3 with elevated hepatic steatosis index) in the absence of other liver diseases.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, multinational estimates of MASLD incidence and its key dynamic predictors. It will shift the epidemiological paradigm from a static to a dynamic model of risk, identifying critical windows for intervention. The findings will directly inform the design of precision public health campaigns aimed at preventing steatosis before it develops.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Younossi and others has excelled in defining prevalence and diagnostic accuracy, this proposal uniquely applies longitudinal, time-varying analytical methods to deconvolute the drivers of new disease incidence, moving beyond the established cross-sectional associations.

proposals.title: Causal Inference for Metabolic Drivers of MASLD Progression: A Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization and Observational Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: The global rise of MASLD is a major public health crisis, yet the causal contributions of specific metabolic traits (e.g., insulin resistance, dyslipidemia patterns, visceral adiposity) versus lifestyle factors (e.g., diet composition, physical inactivity) remain entangled in observational studies. Pre-2026, Dr. Younossi's work has highlighted the diagnostic and economic burden of MASLD and its advanced stages, but a causal framework is needed to prioritize intervention targets. Clarifying these drivers is essential for moving beyond association to effective, personalized prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetically proxied insulin resistance and a specific dyslipidemia signature (high triglycerides, low HDL) are primary causal drivers of MASLD progression to advanced fibrosis, while the effects of lifestyle factors like sedentary behavior are largely mediated through these metabolic pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-sample Mendelian randomization (MR) framework using publicly available pre-2026 genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics for metabolic traits, lifestyle factors, and MASLD phenotypes (including steatosis and fibrosis stages). Primary analysis will use inverse-variance weighted MR, supplemented by MR-Egger and weighted median methods to assess pleiotropy. We will then validate and contextualize these causal estimates within Dr. Younossi's existing multinational patient cohorts, using advanced multivariate regression and mediation analysis to quantify the proportion of lifestyle effects mediated by metabolic intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a ranked, evidence-based list of causal metabolic drivers for MASLD severity, directly informing biomarker and therapeutic target development. It will disentangle the direct effects of lifestyle from those mediated by metabolism, offering a mechanistic model for disease progression. The findings will advance the field from descriptive risk association to a causal understanding of MASLD pathogenesis.
proposals.novelty: While MR has been applied to NAFLD/MASLD risk, its focused use to dissect drivers of fibrosis progression within a comprehensive framework that integrates genetic causality with detailed patient-level phenotypic data is novel. This approach moves beyond establishing a causal link for single factors to modeling the causal network between lifestyle, metabolic intermediates, and advanced liver disease.

proposals.title: Modeling the Impact of Public Health Interventions on the Future Burden of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD): A Simulation Study
proposals.motivation: The global prevalence of MASLD is rising in parallel with obesity and type 2 diabetes (T2D), creating a massive future burden of advanced liver disease, mortality, and healthcare costs. While the clinical risk factors are well-established, there is a critical gap in understanding how population-level interventions could alter this trajectory, which is essential for informing cost-effective public health policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrete, realistic public health interventions targeting key metabolic drivers (e.g., obesity, T2D incidence) will have a non-linear, synergistic effect on reducing the projected long-term prevalence of MASLD and its associated liver-related mortality.
proposals.method: We will develop a state-transition microsimulation model using pre-2026 epidemiological data. The model will simulate a virtual cohort, tracking transitions between health states (no MASLD, MASLD, MASH, advanced fibrosis, decompensation, liver-related death) based on age, sex, BMI, and T2D status. We will calibrate the model using established natural history data. Hypothetical interventions (e.g., population-wide BMI reduction, decreased T2D incidence) will be modeled by altering transition probabilities. Sensitivity analyses will test the robustness of findings.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimates of how specific public health strategies could mitigate the future MASLD epidemic, moving beyond descriptive projections. It will deliver actionable evidence for policymakers by ranking the potential effectiveness of different intervention targets on liver-specific outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has projected MASLD burden, this study uniquely models the *counterfactual* impact of upstream metabolic interventions, directly linking public health action to downstream liver-related mortality, a novel application of simulation modeling in hepatology.

proposals.title: Prognostic Validation of the EDAS Score for Long-Term Hepatic Outcomes in a Multi-Ethnic MASLD Cohort
proposals.motivation: The Early Detection of Advanced Steatosis (EDAS) score is a promising non-invasive tool for identifying significant steatosis, but its long-term prognostic value for predicting disease progression or regression in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is unknown. Validating a simple, accessible score like EDAS against hard clinical endpoints would address a critical gap in risk stratification, enabling more efficient patient management and resource allocation in the face of the global MASLD epidemic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that baseline and longitudinal changes in the EDAS score are independently associated with the long-term risks of MASLD progression (to advanced fibrosis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma), regression, and liver-related clinical events (decompensation, transplantation, death).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective longitudinal cohort study using data from established MASLD registries (e.g., NASH CRN, European cohorts). Baseline and serial EDAS scores (calculated from age, diabetes status, BMI, triglycerides, and ALT) will be linked to long-term outcomes over ≥5 years. We will use time-to-event analyses (Cox proportional hazards models) to assess EDAS as a predictor, adjusting for established risk factors (e.g., FIB-4, NFS). Machine learning techniques, specifically random survival forests, will be employed to evaluate if EDAS adds incremental prognostic value beyond existing non-invasive tests and to identify optimal EDAS thresholds for risk stratification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale evidence on the prognostic utility of the EDAS score, potentially establishing it as a low-cost, routine tool for long-term monitoring in MASLD. It will clarify whether EDAS can identify patients at high risk for progression who require more intensive surveillance versus those with stable disease, directly informing clinical practice guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validated EDAS for cross-sectional steatosis detection, this study uniquely investigates its longitudinal prognostic capability for hard clinical outcomes, integrating it with advanced survival machine learning models to refine dynamic risk prediction in MASLD.

proposals.title: Adaptation and Validation of the Enhanced Liver Fibrosis (ELF) Score Algorithm for Diabetes (ELF-D): A Tool for Stratifying Microvascular Complication Risk in Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Type 2 diabetes (T2D) and obesity are central to the metabolic syndrome, driving not only MASLD but also a high burden of microvascular complications. Pre-2026, risk stratification for diabetic nephropathy and retinopathy relies on individual biomarkers (e.g., albuminuria, HbA1c), lacking integrated, fibrosis-like algorithms that capture the systemic fibro-inflammatory state. The Enhanced Liver Fibrosis (ELF) test, a validated panel of extracellular matrix markers (hyaluronic acid, TIMP-1, PIIINP), predicts advanced fibrosis in MASLD and could model a similar approach for T2D complications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an algorithm derived from the ELF score components, recalibrated with T2D-specific clinical variables, will outperform existing single biomarkers in stratifying patients at high risk for progressive diabetic kidney disease and retinopathy over a 5-year period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using stored serum samples and longitudinal clinical data from established T2D cohorts (e.g., from prior MASLD studies). The original ELF algorithm will be modified by replacing liver-specific weighting with T2D-relevant covariates (duration of diabetes, HbA1c variability, blood pressure) using multivariate Cox regression. The primary outcome is a composite of incident advanced diabetic kidney disease (eGFR decline >40% or progression to macroalbuminuria) or sight-threatening retinopathy. Model performance (ELF-D vs. standard care) will be assessed using time-dependent AUC and net reclassification improvement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for early identification of T2D patients at highest risk for microvascular complications, enabling targeted monitoring and intervention. It will demonstrate the translational potential of a fibrosis-based biomarker panel from hepatology to broader metabolic disease management, reinforcing the shared pathophysiology across organ systems.
proposals.novelty: While ELF is established in hepatology, its systematic adaptation and algorithmic recalibration for diabetes complication risk is novel. This moves beyond simply measuring the same biomarkers in a new population to creating a dedicated, validated clinical decision support tool for endocrinology.

proposals.title: A Multinational Case-Control Study of Genetic, Dietary, and Healthcare Determinants of MASLD Prevalence Disparities: Eastern Europe vs. Sub-Saharan Africa
proposals.motivation: Significant geographic disparities in MASLD prevalence exist, with Eastern Europe reporting among the highest global rates and Sub-Saharan Africa among the lowest. The drivers of this disparity are poorly understood but are critical for developing targeted public health and clinical strategies. This research directly addresses a key knowledge gap in the global epidemiology of metabolic liver disease, moving beyond descriptive prevalence studies to identify causative factors.
proposals.hypothesis: The high prevalence of MASLD in Eastern Europe compared to Africa is driven by a combination of a higher polygenic risk burden for steatosis and fibrosis, distinct dietary patterns (e.g., higher intake of processed foods and fructose-sweetened beverages), and systemic differences in healthcare access affecting early diagnosis and management of metabolic comorbidities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multinational, matched case-control study. Cases (MASLD with advanced fibrosis, F2-F4) and controls (no MASLD) will be recruited from tertiary centers in Poland/Romania (Eastern Europe) and Nigeria/Kenya (Africa), matched for age, sex, and T2D status. All participants will undergo: 1) Genotyping for established MASLD/MASH risk alleles (e.g., PNPLA3, TM6SF2, MBOAT7) and calculation of a polygenic risk score (PRS); 2) Detailed dietary assessment via validated, culturally adapted food frequency questionnaires; 3) Structured interviews on healthcare access (e.g., routine screening, specialist availability). Multivariable logistic regression will model MASLD risk, adjusting for confounders (BMI, physical activity) and testing for region-specific interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated analysis of genetic, lifestyle, and systemic healthcare factors contributing to a major global MASLD disparity. It will generate region-specific risk profiles, informing tailored prevention programs. The findings will advance the field from association to mechanistic understanding of geographic risk, supporting precision public health approaches for liver disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe prevalence differences, this is the first to directly compare high- and low-risk regions using a unified, multi-factorial case-control design. It uniquely integrates genetic PRS with granular dietary data and healthcare system metrics within a single analytical framework to dissect disparity drivers.

proposals.title: Temporal Trends in the Global Prevalence of MASLD in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes: An Updated Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis (2015-2024)
proposals.motivation: The global prevalence of MASLD in patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D) is alarmingly high, with meta-analyses up to 2021 suggesting a rising trend. This confluence of metabolic diseases drives significant liver-related morbidity, including advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis, imposing a substantial clinical and economic burden. Confirming whether this prevalence continues to rise post-2021 is critical for projecting healthcare needs and prioritizing public health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the global prevalence of MASLD in patients with T2D has continued to increase from 2021 through 2024. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this trend is consistent across geographic regions and is most pronounced in subgroups with higher obesity rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies published from January 2022 to December 2024 that report the prevalence of MASLD (diagnosed by imaging or histology) in adult T2D populations. Following PRISMA guidelines, we will search PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library. Data extraction will include study characteristics and prevalence estimates. We will perform a random-effects meta-analysis to pool prevalence estimates overall and by pre-specified time intervals (e.g., 2022-2023, 2024). Temporal trends will be analyzed using meta-regression with publication year as a continuous predictor, adjusting for study-level covariates like diagnostic method and region.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the most current, evidence-based estimate of the global MASLD burden in the high-risk T2D population, directly informing clinical guidelines and resource allocation. It will determine if the previously observed upward trajectory has persisted, plateaued, or accelerated in the post-2021 era, offering crucial data for predictive modeling of future liver disease epidemics.
proposals.novelty: While prior meta-analyses established the baseline, this work is novel in its explicit focus on analyzing temporal trends in the post-2021 period, a critical gap as the MASLD epidemic evolves. The analysis will leverage the updated MASLD nomenclature and diagnostic criteria to provide a contemporary, standardized assessment.

proposals.title: The Impact of the MASLD/MASH Nomenclature Shift on Disease Prevalence and Clinical Risk Stratification: A Multi-Cohort Retrospective Analysis
proposals.motivation: The 2023 multi-society consensus introduced the new terms MASLD and MASH, replacing NAFLD/NASH, to better reflect metabolic dysfunction as the primary driver. This fundamental change in diagnostic criteria, moving from a diagnosis of exclusion to one of inclusion based on cardiometabolic risk factors, has immediate and critical implications for epidemiological reporting and clinical trial recruitment. It is unknown how this shift affects the reported prevalence of the disease in key subpopulations (e.g., with/without T2D, across BMI categories) and the identification of patients at risk for advanced fibrosis, creating uncertainty for public health planning and clinical management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the application of the MASLD criteria will identify a larger patient population compared to the NAFLD criteria, but that this expanded cohort will have a lower overall proportion of patients at high risk for advanced fibrosis, thereby altering the clinical and economic burden estimates of the disease.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective analysis of existing multinational cohorts (e.g., NHANES, UK Biobank, and clinical databases from prior studies) with available metabolic and liver data. For each participant, we will apply both the historical NAFLD (imaging/biomarker-proven steatosis without other causes) and new MASLD (steatosis plus at least one cardiometabolic criterion) definitions. We will calculate and compare prevalence estimates. Subsequently, we will apply validated non-invasive fibrosis tests (FIB-4, NFS, liver stiffness measurement by VCTE where available) to both definitional cohorts to compare the distribution of fibrosis risk stages. Statistical analysis will use paired tests (McNemar's) for prevalence comparison and logistic regression to model predictors of discordant classification.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quantitative assessment of the epidemiological and clinical impact of the nomenclature change, directly informing healthcare systems and policymakers about potential shifts in disease burden. It will clarify whether the new criteria more efficiently capture patients at risk for progressive liver disease or dilute the high-risk population, guiding the design of future screening programs and clinical trials.
proposals.novelty: While the nomenclature change is recognized, no pre-2026 study has systematically quantified its effect on prevalence and risk stratification across diverse, real-world populations, moving beyond expert opinion to data-driven analysis of its practical consequences.

proposals.title: Real-World Comparative Effectiveness of GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and SGLT2 Inhibitors on Hepatic Decompensation Risk in Patients with MASLD and Type 2 Diabetes: A Target Trial Emulation Study
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, while GLP-1 RAs and SGLT2 inhibitors show promise for MASLD in trials, their real-world comparative effectiveness on hard clinical endpoints like hepatic decompensation remains poorly characterized. This is a critical evidence gap for clinicians managing the high-risk intersection of MASLD and type 2 diabetes, where treatment decisions must balance hepatic, metabolic, and cardiovascular risks.
proposals.hypothesis: In a real-world population with MASLD and type 2 diabetes, initiation of GLP-1 receptor agonists, compared to initiation of SGLT2 inhibitors, is associated with a reduced risk of incident hepatic decompensation events over long-term follow-up.
proposals.method: We will design a target trial emulation using large U.S. administrative claims and electronic health record databases (e.g., Optum, TriNetX). Patients with MASLD and type 2 diabetes initiating a GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor will be identified. We will apply high-dimensional propensity score matching to balance >100 baseline covariates. The primary outcome is a composite of hepatic decompensation events (ascites, variceal hemorrhage, hepatic encephalopathy). We will use an intention-to-treat Cox model and a per-protocol analysis using inverse probability of censoring weighting to account for treatment discontinuation and switching.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, real-world comparative effectiveness evidence on two leading drug classes for preventing advanced liver disease in MASLD. It will inform clinical guidelines and payer decisions by quantifying the potential hepatic benefit of these agents beyond glycemic control. The methodological framework will establish a template for robust pharmacoepidemiologic studies of MASLD therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: This applies the rigorous, causal inference-focused framework of target trial emulation—common in cardiovascular pharmacoepidemiology but novel to MASLD research—to compare drug effects on liver-specific outcomes, moving beyond surrogate biomarkers to clinically meaningful endpoints.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of an Individualized Risk Prediction Model for MASLD in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes
proposals.motivation: Type 2 diabetes (T2D) is a major risk factor for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) and its progression to advanced fibrosis, yet risk is heterogeneous. Existing population-level risk scores lack the granularity to provide actionable, individualized risk stratification for a single patient in a clinical encounter. An accurate, personalized prediction tool is urgently needed to guide targeted screening and preventive interventions in this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating routine clinical, biochemical, and anthropometric data can accurately predict the presence of significant liver steatosis (MASLD) at the individual patient level in a T2D cohort.
proposals.method: We will develop a predictive model using retrospective, multi-center patient data from established MASLD/T2D cohorts. Individual-level features will include demographics, diabetes duration/control (HbA1c), lipid profiles, liver enzymes, and anthropometrics. We will compare the performance of several pre-2026 machine learning algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), using liver imaging (ultrasound or controlled attenuation parameter) as the gold-standard outcome for steatosis. Model performance will be evaluated via cross-validation and on a held-out test set using AUC, calibration plots, and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically deployable tool that provides a personalized probability of MASLD for a T2D patient, moving beyond population averages. It will directly inform point-of-care decisions regarding the need for further liver-specific evaluation. The study will also identify key modifiable risk drivers at the individual level, offering targets for personalized intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies MASLD risk factors in T2D populations, this proposal focuses explicitly on individual-level probabilistic prediction for a single patient, a critical shift from epidemiological association to clinical decision support. The comparative framework of interpretable and black-box models will address both accuracy and clinical usability.

proposals.title: Comparative Therapeutic Efficacy in NAFLD vs. MASLD: A Post-Hoc Analysis of Clinical Trial Cohorts to Assess Interchangeability
proposals.motivation: The 2023 redefinition from NAFLD to MASLD has created a new diagnostic paradigm, but its clinical impact on patient management and trial interpretation remains unclear. Given the extensive pre-2026 literature and trial data under the NAFLD framework, it is critical to determine if the two definitions identify populations with identical therapeutic responses, which is essential for validating the use of historical data and ensuring continuity in clinical practice and drug development.
proposals.hypothesis: Despite differences in diagnostic criteria, cohorts defined by NAFLD and MASLD will demonstrate statistically equivalent therapeutic responses to pharmacologic and lifestyle interventions, confirming their clinical interchangeability.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational analysis of patient-level data from completed Phase 3 and 4 NAFLD/MASH trials (e.g., using drugs like obeticholic acid, resmetirom, pioglitazone, and GLP-1 agonists). Patients will be re-classified into NAFLD (pre-2023 criteria) and MASLD (2023 criteria) groups. The primary outcome will be the relative change in liver fat content (by MRI-PDFF) and resolution of NASH without worsening of fibrosis. We will use propensity score matching to create balanced cohorts and employ mixed-effects models and equivalence testing (using two one-sided tests, TOST) to compare treatment effects between the redefined groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative evidence on whether the NAFLD-to-MASLD transition is merely a nomenclature change or if it meaningfully alters therapeutic cohort definitions. It will offer crucial guidance for meta-analyses, regulatory review of historical trial data, and the design of future clinical studies in metabolic liver disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has compared the epidemiological overlap of NAFLD and MASLD, no study has specifically tested the equivalence of their therapeutic responses using advanced statistical equivalence testing on interventional trial data, which is the ultimate test of clinical interchangeability.

proposals.title: Comparative Impact of MASLD Definitions on Patient-Centered Outcomes: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis of Liver-Specific Morbidity and Quality of Life
proposals.motivation: The clinical definition of MASLD (Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease) has shifted focus from histology to cardiometabolic risk factors. However, the primary validation for these definitions has relied on associations with all-cause mortality, which is heavily influenced by competing cardiovascular and oncological risks. For hepatologists and patients, liver-specific morbidity, progression to advanced fibrosis, and health-related quality of life (HRQL) are more directly relevant endpoints for evaluating disease definitions and guiding clinical management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when MASLD definitions are evaluated against liver-specific clinical endpoints (e.g., hepatic decompensation, progression to advanced fibrosis, liver transplantation) and patient-reported HRQL, their discriminatory performance and clinical utility will differ significantly from their performance against all-cause mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational cohort study using established databases (e.g., NHANES-linked mortality, tertiary center cohorts) of patients with steatosis. We will apply competing risk regression models (Fine-Gray) to analyze time-to-event data for liver-specific morbidity (decompensation, HCC, liver-related death) and liver transplantation, treating non-liver death as a competing risk. HRQL will be assessed using validated instruments (e.g., CLDQ-NAFLD) where available. We will compare the predictive accuracy (C-statistics, net reclassification improvement) of the MASLD definition against its predecessor (NAFLD) for these liver-centric outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a patient-centered framework for validating liver disease definitions, directly informing clinical trial design and resource allocation. It will quantify the real-world hepatological burden captured by MASLD criteria, moving beyond mortality driven by extra-hepatic causes. The results will guide clinicians on which definition best identifies patients at risk for progressive liver disease requiring specialist care.
proposals.novelty: This work challenges the paradigm of using all-cause mortality as the primary validation endpoint for a liver-specific disease definition. It innovatively applies competing risk analysis to isolate liver-related outcomes and integrates patient-reported quality of life, a critical yet often neglected dimension in nosological studies.

proposals.title: Prospective Validation of the Enhanced Liver Fibrosis (ELF) Test, FIB-4, and Transient Elastography for Staging Fibrosis in Strictly Defined Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) Cohorts
proposals.motivation: The 2023 redefinition from NAFLD to MASLD created a new, more specific diagnostic paradigm based on cardiometabolic risk factors. However, the performance of established non-invasive tests (NITs) like ELF, FIB-4, and transient elastography (TE) has not been prospectively validated in cohorts defined strictly by these new criteria. This creates a critical evidence gap, as clinicians now need reliable, validated tools to risk-stratify and manage fibrosis in the pure MASLD population without confounding from other liver diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the diagnostic accuracy of ELF, FIB-4, and TE for detecting advanced fibrosis (≥F3) will be superior in a prospectively enrolled cohort defined by pure MASLD criteria compared to historical NAFLD cohorts, due to the exclusion of patients with other etiologies of liver disease.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multinational, observational study enrolling adults with imaging-confirmed hepatic steatosis who meet the MASLD criteria (presence of ≥1 of 5 cardiometabolic risk factors) and have no evidence of other chronic liver diseases. All participants will concurrently undergo the three index tests (serum ELF, FIB-4 calculation, and VCTE) and liver biopsy as the reference standard, interpreted centrally by blinded pathologists using the NASH CRN system. Diagnostic performance will be assessed using AUROC, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values, with subgroup analyses by age, diabetes status, and obesity class.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence (prospective, biopsy-validated) on the real-world accuracy of the most clinically relevant NITs in the pure MASLD era. It will deliver practice-ready cut-off values and test sequencing algorithms, directly informing clinical guidelines and improving non-invasive risk stratification for liver-related outcomes in this large patient population.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies validated NITs in heterogeneous NAFLD populations, this is the first prospective validation study designed explicitly for the new MASLD definition, eliminating diagnostic contamination from other liver diseases and providing clarity on test performance in the contemporary diagnostic framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Impact of the MASLD Diagnostic Criteria on Clinical Trial Cohort Composition and Generalizability: A Retrospective Analysis of NAFLD Trial Populations
proposals.motivation: The 2023 redefinition from NAFLD to MASLD, requiring at least one cardiometabolic criterion, fundamentally alters the patient population defined by the disease. This shift has critical, unquantified implications for clinical trial design and interpretation. If historical NAFLD trial cohorts differ significantly from MASLD-defined populations, it could challenge the applicability of prior evidence to current and future MASLD patients, impacting drug development and clinical guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying MASLD criteria to historical NAFLD clinical trial cohorts will identify a significant subset of trial-eligible patients who would be excluded under MASLD definitions, and that this excluded subgroup will have distinct clinical and prognostic profiles, particularly regarding fibrosis progression and cardiovascular risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational cohort study using pooled, de-identified individual patient data from major NAFLD/MASLD clinical trials and registries (pre-2023). For each patient, we will apply the 2023 MASLD diagnostic criteria. We will use multivariable logistic regression to identify baseline characteristics (e.g., fibrosis stage, metabolic features, demographics) associated with MASLD inclusion vs. exclusion. Survival analysis (Kaplan-Meier, Cox proportional hazards) will compare long-term hepatic (fibrosis progression, decompensation) and extra-hepatic (cardiovascular events) outcomes between groups, adjusting for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence quantifying how the MASLD redefinition alters clinical trial populations. It will inform sponsors and regulators on potential biases when extrapolating from older NAFLD trials and guide the design of future MASLD trials to ensure enrolled cohorts are representative of the newly defined disease population. The results will clarify whether the MASLD criteria successfully select a higher-risk phenotype.
proposals.novelty: While the conceptual shift to MASLD is recognized, no study has systematically analyzed its practical impact on the composition and risk profiles of patients eligible for interventional trials. This moves beyond diagnostic accuracy to directly address a critical translational gap in clinical research methodology for this highly prevalent liver disease.

proposals.title: Genetic Epidemiology of MASLD: A Polygenic Risk Score Analysis to Validate the Metabolic Distinction from Non-MASLD NAFLD
proposals.motivation: The redefinition of NAFLD to MASLD emphasizes a metabolic underpinning, but it remains unclear if this new definition captures a biologically distinct entity. Pre-2026, genetic studies have identified variants (e.g., PNPLA3, TM6SF2) associated with NAFLD progression, but these have not been systematically applied to test the validity of the MASLD vs. non-MASLD classification. Validating this distinction is critical for accurate risk stratification and targeted therapy development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the genetic architecture, as quantified by polygenic risk scores (PRS) derived from established NAFLD-associated loci, will differ significantly between patients meeting MASLD criteria and those with non-MASLD NAFLD, supporting MASLD as a genetically distinct subtype.
proposals.method: We will assemble a multi-ethnic cohort with biopsy-proven NAFLD, phenotyped for metabolic criteria per the MASLD definition. Using pre-2026 genome-wide association study (GWAS) summary statistics for NAFLD and related traits (e.g., liver fat, fibrosis), we will construct PRS using established methods like PRS-CS or LDpred2. We will compare PRS distributions between MASLD and non-MASLD groups using logistic regression, adjusting for age, sex, and genetic ancestry. Secondary analyses will test PRS associations with histological severity (steatosis, inflammation, fibrosis) within each subgroup.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first genetic epidemiology evidence validating (or refuting) the MASLD definition. It will clarify whether metabolic criteria align with underlying genetic risk, informing future diagnostic pathways and clinical trial design. The results will directly address the debate on whether MASLD is a distinct disease entity or a semantic reframing.
proposals.novelty: While PRS are established in NAFLD risk prediction, their application to test the validity of a new clinical definition is novel. This moves beyond association to explicitly test a nosological hypothesis central to the field's redefinition efforts.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Outcomes and Risk Stratification in Lean MASLD: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) in non-obese individuals (lean MASLD) is increasingly recognized but remains poorly characterized. Pre-2026 evidence suggests these patients may have a distinct metabolic profile and potentially a different risk for disease progression, yet they are often overlooked in clinical trials and risk prediction models focused on obesity. Understanding the natural history and drivers of advanced fibrosis in this subgroup is critical for equitable and effective patient management.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lean MASLD patients exhibit a unique clinical phenotype and progression trajectory, where risk for advanced fibrosis and hepatic decompensation is driven by specific metabolic and genetic factors distinct from those in obese MASLD.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective-prospective cohort study using data from multinational registries (e.g., NASH CRN, European cohorts) to identify patients with biopsy-proven MASLD and BMI <25 kg/m² (lean) matched 1:2 by age and sex with obese MASLD (BMI ≥30). The primary outcome is time to composite endpoint of histological progression to advanced fibrosis (F3-F4), hepatic decompensation, or liver-related mortality. We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to identify independent risk factors (e.g., PNPLA3 genotype, dyslipidemia, insulin resistance patterns) and develop a lean-specific risk prediction model using machine learning (LASSO regression for variable selection, followed by random survival forest analysis for model building and validation).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal characterization of fibrosis progression in lean MASLD, defining its distinct clinical course. It will yield a validated, lean-specific risk stratification tool to guide surveillance and intervention, addressing a critical gap in current MASLD management paradigms that are predominantly obesity-centric.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe the prevalence of lean MASLD, this proposal uniquely focuses on its long-term histological and clinical outcomes and develops a dedicated risk prediction model, moving beyond cross-sectional description to actionable prognostication for this understudied population.

proposals.title: Community-Embedded Phenotyping and Intervention in MENA Populations: A Data-Driven Strategy to Refine MASLD Diagnostic and Risk Stratification Criteria
proposals.motivation: MASLD prevalence is exceptionally high in MENA populations, yet current diagnostic criteria and risk stratification models are largely derived from Western cohorts. This gap is critical, as pre-2026 work by Younossi and others highlights significant variability in the performance of non-invasive tests across ethnicities and metabolic subgroups. Without population-specific validation, there is a risk of misclassification and suboptimal prevention strategies in these high-burden communities.
proposals.hypothesis: Community-level participatory phenotyping will reveal distinct metabolic, anthropometric, and imaging profiles in MENA populations with MASLD, enabling the identification of ethnic-specific diagnostic thresholds (e.g., for ALT, CAP, or FIB-4) and risk factors that improve the accuracy of case identification and fibrosis prediction compared to current universal criteria.
proposals.method: We will establish prospective, community-based prevention pilots in 2-3 high-prevalence MENA communities. Participants will undergo detailed phenotyping including anthropometrics, advanced metabolic profiling (HOMA-IR, adipokines, novel lipid particles), vibration-controlled transient elastography (VCTE) with CAP, and collection of lifestyle/socioeconomic data. Data will be analyzed using machine learning (e.g., random forest/elastic net regression) to identify key predictors of steatosis and fibrosis. We will test the diagnostic accuracy of current thresholds and iteratively derive optimized, population-specific cut-offs using ROC analysis, embedding this validation within the ongoing intervention.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first large-scale, granular phenotypic dataset for MASLD in MENA communities, directly testing the 'one-size-fits-all' diagnostic approach. It will produce actionable, evidence-based proposals for refining MASLD criteria for this population. Furthermore, it establishes a model for community-engaged research that integrates intervention with real-time biomarker validation.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond observational critique, this study actively tests and refines diagnostic criteria within an interventional framework. It leverages community participation not just for recruitment, but as an integrated platform for concurrent data generation and strategy refinement, a methodologically distinct approach to addressing ethnic disparities in MASLD.

proposals.title: Causal Decomposition of Type 2 Diabetes on MASLD Risk in MENA Populations: A G-Computation Analysis of Direct and Mediated Effects
proposals.motivation: In the pre-2026 context of the global MASLD epidemic, the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region faces a disproportionately high burden. While Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) is a strong associative risk factor, it remains unclear whether T2D exerts a direct causal effect on MASLD or if its influence is primarily mediated through co-occurring metabolic dysfunctions like dyslipidemia and hypertension. Disentangling this is critical for developing targeted prevention and management strategies in this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of the total causal effect of T2D on incident advanced MASLD (MASH with significant fibrosis) in MENA cohorts is mediated through intermediate metabolic variables, and that the controlled direct effect of T2D, independent of these mediators, will be substantially attenuated.
proposals.method: Using longitudinal cohort data from MENA (e.g., Golestan Cohort, UAE Healthy Future Study), we will construct a pre-specified causal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). We will apply parametric g-computation (a standardization-based causal inference method) to estimate the total effect of T2D on MASLD risk and the controlled direct effect when fixing mediators (dyslipidemia, hypertension, central obesity) to pre-specified levels. Sensitivity analyses using methods like E-values will quantify robustness to unmeasured confounding. Analyses will be stratified by key subgroups (age, sex, obesity status).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causally-informed hierarchy of metabolic drivers for MASLD in the MENA region, moving beyond prediction to quantification of direct effects. It will clarify if T2D management alone is sufficient to mitigate MASLD risk or if concurrent control of specific mediators is paramount. The findings will directly inform precision public health and clinical trial design for this population.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in applying formal causal mediation methods (g-computation) to the MASLD field within the understudied MENA population, explicitly testing the causal pathway rather than relying on associative models. It directly addresses the conceptual question of whether T2D is a 'disease' driver or a correlate ('the emperor's new clothes') for MASLD risk.

proposals.title: Comparative Predictive Power of Metabolic Dysfunctions for MASLD and Advanced Fibrosis in the MENA Population: A Focus on Type 2 Diabetes, Dyslipidemia, and Obesity
proposals.motivation: The global MASLD epidemic is strongly linked to metabolic syndrome, with Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) considered a primary driver of disease progression. However, the unique genetic, dietary, and lifestyle factors in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region, which has among the highest global rates of obesity and T2D, may alter the hierarchy of metabolic risk factors. Pre-2026, it remains unclear if other metabolic dysfunctions, such as atherogenic dyslipidemia or central obesity, are stronger predictors of MASLD and fibrosis than T2D in this specific population, impacting risk stratification and public health strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: In the MENA population, metabolic dysfunctions other than T2D—specifically, atherogenic dyslipidemia (high triglycerides, low HDL) and measures of central adiposity—are stronger independent predictors of prevalent MASLD and advanced fibrosis (F2+) than T2D status alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multinational, cross-sectional analysis of existing and prospectively collected cohorts from MENA countries (e.g., Egypt, Saudi Arabia, Iran). All participants will undergo vibration-controlled transient elastography (FibroScan®) with CAP for steatosis and fibrosis assessment. Using multivariate logistic regression models, we will evaluate the independent association of T2D, HOMA-IR, lipid profiles, BMI, and waist-to-hip ratio with MASLD (CAP ≥248 dB/m) and advanced fibrosis (LSM ≥8.2 kPa). Model performance and predictor hierarchy will be assessed using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) and dominance analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of metabolic risk factors for MASLD in the MENA region, clarifying whether regional prevention guidelines should prioritize glycemic control or lipid/weight management. It will generate a region-specific predictive model for advanced fibrosis risk, moving beyond Western-centric paradigms. The findings will inform targeted public health interventions and personalized monitoring strategies for high-risk subgroups in MENA.
