proposals.novelty: While T2D is universally recognized as a key MASLD driver, this study challenges its primacy in a high-risk, understudied population, testing the novel hypothesis that the regional metabolic phenotype may confer a different hierarchy of risk. It applies rigorous, pre-2026 non-invasive diagnostic methods in a direct, comparative framework not previously executed in MENA.

proposals.title: Disparities in MASLD Burden and Fibrosis Risk: A Targeted mHealth Screening Study of High-Risk Occupational and Rural Populations
proposals.motivation: Current estimates of MASLD prevalence and fibrosis risk are derived from general or clinical populations, potentially masking significant disparities in underserved subgroups. Pre-2026 evidence links occupational exposures, circadian disruption, and rural healthcare access to metabolic risk, but systematic screening in these high-risk, hard-to-reach populations is lacking. This gap hinders the development of equitable public health strategies for MASLD, a disease with substantial economic and clinical burden.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted mHealth screening will reveal a significantly higher prevalence of clinically significant MASLD (steatosis with elevated FIB-4) in high-risk occupational and rural populations compared to general population estimates, and that distinct, modifiable risk factor profiles will be identified within these subgroups.
proposals.method: We will deploy a validated, adapted mHealth platform (e.g., integrating the FIB-4 index calculator and a MASLD risk score like the MAFLD score) via a secure mobile app to screen consenting adults from targeted cohorts: shift workers in manufacturing, long-haul transport drivers, and residents of geographically isolated rural counties. The platform will collect demographic data, validated risk questionnaires, and key clinical inputs (age, AST, ALT, platelets via point-of-care testing or recent records) to compute FIB-4 and risk scores. Data will be aggregated in a cloud-based analytics dashboard using pre-2026 statistical methods (multivariable logistic regression, geospatial mapping) to compare prevalence and identify subgroup-specific risk factor associations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first granular, real-world data map of MASLD burden in specific high-risk subgroups, moving beyond national averages. It will generate actionable evidence on disparities to inform targeted screening guidelines and advocacy for resource allocation. The findings will also elucidate novel, population-specific risk factor associations to refine etiological models of MASLD progression.
proposals.novelty: This transforms mHealth from a tool for broad surveillance into a targeted mechanism for health equity research in MASLD, explicitly testing the hypothesis that general estimates mask severe disparities. The focused integration of occupational and rural geography as primary sampling frames, rather than confounders, is a novel approach to understanding disease distribution.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease in Middle Eastern and North African Youth: A Prospective Cohort Study of Onset Age and Progression Risk
proposals.motivation: The global MASLD epidemic is escalating, yet its natural history in younger, understudied populations like those in the MENA region—a region with high prevalence of metabolic risk factors—remains poorly characterized. Pre-2026, most longitudinal data derive from older Western cohorts, creating a critical knowledge gap regarding whether early-onset disease follows a more aggressive fibrotic course. Understanding life-course trajectories in this high-risk demographic is essential for timely, targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MASLD with onset in adolescence or young adulthood in the MENA population follows a distinct and more rapid progression trajectory to advanced fibrosis compared to later-onset disease, driven by a unique interaction of genetic, metabolic, and environmental exposures.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, multi-center MENA cohort of 800 participants, stratified by age at detection (adolescents: 12-18y, young adults: 19-30y, later-onset: 40-55y). Baseline assessment includes vibration-controlled transient elastography (FibroScan® with CAP) for steatosis and fibrosis, detailed metabolic phenotyping, genotyping for PNPLA3 and TM6SF2 variants, and standardized environmental/lifestyle questionnaires. Participants will undergo annual follow-up for 5 years. Trajectory analysis will use group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) and joint longitudinal-survival models to identify distinct progression clusters and model fibrosis stage over time as a function of baseline predictors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal data on MASLD progression in MENA youth, defining age-specific risk trajectories. It will generate a predictive model integrating genetic and metabolic factors to identify youth at highest risk for rapid fibrosis progression, enabling stratified prevention strategies. The findings will directly inform regional clinical guidelines for screening and management in younger populations.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond cross-sectional prevalence studies to prospectively define life-course disease trajectories in a genetically distinct, high-risk, and understudied population. It uniquely fuses longitudinal imaging-based fibrosis staging with genetic and environmental data to model progression from the point of early-onset detection, a critical pre-cirrhotic window for intervention.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Trajectories of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) and Fibrosis Progression in a Multi-Center Middle East and North Africa (MENA) Cohort
proposals.motivation: The MENA region has among the highest global prevalence of MASLD, driven by epidemics of obesity and type 2 diabetes, yet longitudinal data on disease progression and fibrosis risk are critically lacking. Prior studies by Younossi et al. have highlighted diagnostic accuracy of non-invasive tests but have not applied them serially to define natural history in this high-risk, understudied population. Understanding the rate and drivers of progression to advanced fibrosis is essential for regional healthcare planning and personalized risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MASLD progression rates and trajectories to advanced fibrosis in the MENA population are distinct and more aggressive than those reported in Western cohorts, driven by a unique interplay of metabolic risk factors, genetic predispositions (e.g., PNPLA3), and regional environmental exposures.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, multi-center cohort across 5-7 tertiary centers in the MENA region, enrolling 2000 adults with MASLD. Participants will undergo biennial assessments including clinical evaluation, laboratory tests, and paired non-invasive tests (NITs): Vibration-Controlled Transient Elastography (FibroScan) for CAP and liver stiffness measurement (LSM), and the FIB-4 score. Progression will be defined as a clinically significant increase in LSM (≥1.5 kPa/year) or transition to a higher risk FIB-4 category. We will use joint longitudinal-survival models (e.g., shared random effects models) to analyze the relationship between time-varying metabolic risk factors (glycemic control, weight change) and fibrosis progression, adjusting for baseline covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, longitudinal characterization of MASLD natural history in the MENA region, quantifying real-world rates of fibrosis progression. It will identify region-specific predictors of rapid progression, informing targeted screening and intervention strategies. The data will be crucial for validating and refining risk prediction models, like the MASLD fibrosis score, for this population.
proposals.novelty: While prior multinational work by the investigator has compared diagnostic accuracy of NITs cross-sectionally, this proposal uniquely applies serial NITs within a longitudinal framework specifically in the MENA region to model disease trajectories, moving beyond prevalence estimates to dynamic risk assessment.

proposals.title: Projecting the Full Socioeconomic Burden of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease in the Middle East and North Africa: A Scenario-Based Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The MENA region has among the highest global prevalence of MASLD, driven by epidemics of obesity and type 2 diabetes, yet its comprehensive economic impact remains poorly quantified. Current studies, including those by our group, often focus on direct healthcare costs of advanced disease, neglecting indirect costs and future projections under demographic and public health trends. A holistic, forward-looking assessment is critical to inform regional health policy and resource allocation for this escalating chronic liver disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the structural framework of climate-economic Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) to MASLD will reveal a substantially higher total socioeconomic burden in the MENA region than estimated by traditional cost-of-illness studies, and that scenario analysis will identify key leverage points for cost-effective intervention.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-country, dynamic simulation model for the MENA region. The model architecture will integrate: 1) an epidemiological module using pre-2026 prevalence and progression data (from steatosis to MASH, advanced fibrosis, and decompensation) stratified by country, age, and metabolic risk factors; 2) a cost module incorporating direct medical costs (from our prior work) and indirect costs (productivity loss, disability weights from GBD studies); and 3) a scenario projection module. We will employ a Markov cohort simulation framework, calibrated with regional data, and run it under different demographic and public health intervention scenarios to 2040.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-scenario, comprehensive estimate of MASLD's lifetime economic burden across the MENA region, quantifying the cost of inaction versus targeted prevention. It will deliver a policy-relevant tool for health ministries to project the long-term economic return on investment for screening and management strategies for MASLD and its metabolic drivers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual and methodological adaptation of scenario-based economic impact modeling from climate science to a chronic non-communicable disease, moving beyond static accounting to a dynamic, systems-based projection of MASLD's full societal cost.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Direct and Indirect Economic Burden of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) Region: A Multi-Country, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The MENA region has among the highest global prevalence of obesity and type 2 diabetes, key drivers of MASLD, yet the region-specific economic impact of MASLD remains largely unquantified. Prior cost-of-illness studies, including Dr. Younossi's work in primary biliary cholangitis, have focused on Western populations, creating a critical evidence gap for healthcare resource planning and policy in MENA health systems, which face unique demographic and economic pressures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the direct healthcare costs and indirect productivity losses associated with MASLD and its advanced fibrosis stages in the MENA region are substantial and increase significantly with disease progression, with costs varying markedly across countries due to differences in healthcare infrastructure and economic development.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using hospital and claims databases from at least three MENA countries (e.g., Saudi Arabia, UAE, Egypt). Patients with MASLD will be identified using ICD-10 codes and stratified by fibrosis stage using non-invasive tests (FIB-4, vibration-controlled transient elastography). Direct medical costs (hospitalizations, procedures, medications) and indirect costs (absenteeism, presenteeism via the Work Productivity and Activity Impairment questionnaire) will be collected over a 3-year follow-up. Generalized linear models (gamma distribution with log link) will be used to analyze cost drivers, adjusting for age, diabetes, obesity, and country.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, multi-country estimate of the economic burden of MASLD in the MENA region, directly informing national health priorities and resource allocation. It will extend Dr. Younossi's health economics research into a high-prevalence, understudied region and establish a framework for ongoing cost surveillance.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel as it moves beyond describing MASLD prevalence in MENA to quantifying its multi-faceted economic impact, integrating direct costs with region-specific productivity losses, and comparing burdens across diverse healthcare economies within MENA, which has not been done before 2026.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways and Dynamic Risk Trajectories of MASLD in a Prospective Middle Eastern and North African (MENA) Cohort: A Gene-Environment Interaction Study
proposals.motivation: The Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region has among the highest global prevalence of MASLD, yet the causal drivers and progression dynamics specific to these ancestries remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Younossi's work on diagnostic accuracy across subgroups, highlight the critical need for etiological research that moves beyond association to identify modifiable risk factors and ancestry-specific intervention points. Establishing a deeply phenotyped MENA cohort is essential to address this gap and inform personalized prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetic risk variants for MASLD interact dynamically with modifiable lifestyle exposures (e.g., diet, physical activity) in a time-dependent manner to causally drive disease progression from steatosis to MASH and fibrosis, with significant effect heterogeneity across MENA ancestries.
proposals.method: We will establish a prospective, deeply phenotyped cohort of 3,000 adults with MASLD from diverse MENA ancestries, with serial assessments over 5 years. First, we will apply two-sample Mendelian Randomization (MR) using established genetic instruments (e.g., PNPLA3, TM6SF2) as proxies for lifestyle factors to test causal effects on disease incidence and severity. Second, we will implement a longitudinal, time-to-event analysis framework using multi-state Markov models and joint models for longitudinal and survival data to track dynamic gene-environment interactions and their impact on progression to MASH and advanced fibrosis (F2+).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal and dynamic model of MASLD progression in a MENA population, identifying ancestry-specific genetic modifiers and critical windows for environmental intervention. It will generate a validated, personalized risk prediction tool for clinical use and establish a foundational biorepository for future mechanistic studies in this high-risk, understudied group.
proposals.novelty: This work integrates causal inference (MR) with dynamic, longitudinal modeling of gene-environment interplay specifically in a MENA cohort, moving beyond the cross-sectional associations that dominate pre-2026 MASLD research. It explicitly tests for ancestry-specific interaction effects, a critical step towards equitable precision medicine.

proposals.title: Iterative Calibration and Validation of an Agent-Based Model for MASLD Policy Evaluation Using a Prospective Longitudinal Cohort
proposals.motivation: The global burden of MASLD is immense, yet evidence to guide effective public health policy remains limited. Current consensus recommendations are based on cross-sectional data and static models, which fail to capture the dynamic, heterogeneous progression of disease and the long-term impact of interventions. Policymakers lack a robust, evidence-based tool to simulate and compare the population-level effectiveness of different strategies for preventing advanced fibrosis, liver-related mortality, and cardiovascular events.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent-based model (ABM), iteratively calibrated and validated against prospective, real-world longitudinal outcome data, will provide a more accurate and dynamic simulation tool for evaluating the long-term clinical and economic impact of MASLD policy interventions than models based solely on static epidemiological inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop an ABM in a platform like NetLogo, where agents (individuals) have attributes (age, sex, BMI, diabetes status) and transition probabilities for disease states (steatosis, MASH, fibrosis stages) derived from pre-2026 consensus statements and meta-analyses. The model will simulate interventions (e.g., diabetes control, weight loss programs). Its long-term predictions for hard endpoints will be continuously tested against data from a newly established, prospective, multi-center longitudinal cohort of MASLD patients, with follow-up for hepatic and cardiovascular events. Model parameters will be refined using Bayesian calibration techniques, creating a continuous validation cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, dynamic policy simulation tool that quantifies the real-world effectiveness of current MASLD management guidelines. It will provide novel, data-driven estimates of the long-term population-level impact of interventions, directly informing cost-effectiveness analyses and priority-setting for health systems burdened by metabolic liver disease.
proposals.novelty: While ABMs exist in epidemiology, their application in hepatology has been limited and rarely involves continuous, prospective validation against a dedicated longitudinal cohort. This fusion of dynamic simulation with iterative real-world data integration represents a novel paradigm for moving from static consensus to adaptive, evidence-driven policy evaluation in MASLD.

proposals.title: An Agent-Based Model to Simulate the Long-Term Health and Economic Impact of Public Health Policies on Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) Progression
proposals.motivation: MASLD is a major global health burden, with progression to advanced fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma driving significant morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs. Pre-2026, policy decisions on interventions (e.g., dietary regulations, screening programs) are made with limited quantitative evidence of their long-term population-level effectiveness. A simulation framework is urgently needed to project outcomes and optimize resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agent-based modeling (ABM) will reveal that early, population-wide interventions targeting metabolic risk factors are more cost-effective in reducing MASLD-related decompensation and mortality over a 30-year horizon than strategies focused solely on high-risk or advanced disease subgroups.
proposals.method: We will develop a discrete-time, stochastic ABM using a modular architecture in Python (Mesa framework). Agents (individuals) will have attributes (age, BMI, diabetes status, fibrosis stage) and transition between health states based on probabilities derived from pre-2026 meta-analyses (e.g., Younossi et al. diagnostic accuracy studies). The model will incorporate specific intervention modules (e.g., sugar tax impact on BMI, non-invasive screening protocols) and track outcomes including fibrosis progression, decompensation events, liver transplants, and costs. Calibration and validation will use real-world cohort data and sensitivity analyses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, dynamic simulation tool to quantify the long-term clinical and economic consequences of MASLD policies. It will directly inform public health stakeholders by identifying the most impactful intervention levers. The model will also serve as a foundational platform for integrating future data on novel biomarkers and therapies.
proposals.novelty: While ABM is used in infectious disease and oncology, its application to MASLD policy is novel. This approach moves beyond static cost-effectiveness analyses by capturing heterogeneous risk profiles, individual behavior, and the dynamic interplay of metabolic comorbidities over a lifetime, which is critical for a complex, lifestyle-driven disease.

proposals.title: A Bayesian Multi-Stream Evidence Synthesis Model to Estimate the Undiagnosed Burden of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD)
proposals.motivation: The true population burden of MASLD is obscured by massive underdiagnosis, hindering accurate healthcare resource planning and public health interventions. Current estimates rely heavily on prevalence models and assumptions about healthcare access, which are non-unique and lack empirical validation from real-world clinical data streams. A robust, data-driven estimate of the undiagnosed population is urgently needed to inform screening strategies and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating multiple, independent empirical data streams through a Bayesian synthesis framework will yield a more accurate and constrained estimate of the undiagnosed MASLD population than traditional inverse modeling or single-source approaches.
proposals.method: We will construct a Bayesian hierarchical model with three primary evidence streams: 1) Unexplained elevated ALT/AST in primary care electronic health records (EHRs), 2) Radiology reports containing terms for hepatic steatosis without corresponding MASLD/MASH ICD codes, and 3) Risk-factor-adjusted prevalence from screened biobank cohorts (e.g., UK Biobank). Traditional prevalence and access-to-care estimates will serve as informative priors. The model will be implemented using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling via Stan to estimate the posterior distribution of the undiagnosed fraction, explicitly quantifying uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically triangulated, national-level estimate of undiagnosed MASLD, with quantified uncertainty. It will deliver a validated, transparent modeling framework that can be updated with new data and adapted to other health systems, directly addressing a critical evidence gap in hepatology and metabolic disease epidemiology.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond using empirical data merely for validation; it treats diverse, imperfect real-world data streams as core evidence to constrain and update prior models, directly solving the non-uniqueness problem of inverse estimates. The multi-stream synthesis from routine care, imaging, and biobanks is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: Estimating the Undiagnosed Burden of MASLD: A Novel Inverse Modeling Approach Integrating Population Prevalence and Healthcare Access Disparities
proposals.motivation: Despite high population prevalence, MASLD remains vastly underdiagnosed in clinical practice, leading to missed opportunities for intervention and risk stratification for advanced fibrosis. Pre-2026 studies, including Dr. Younossi's work, highlight the diagnostic gaps across subgroups like T2DM and obesity, but a quantitative, population-level estimate of the undiagnosed caseload is lacking. This gap hinders public health planning and resource allocation for this burgeoning metabolic liver disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inversely modeling the relationship between true disease prevalence, healthcare system access factors, and clinically recorded diagnosis rates, we can generate a robust, stratified estimate of the number of undiagnosed MASLD cases in key at-risk populations.
proposals.method: We will employ a Bayesian inverse problem framework. Inputs will include: 1) high-quality, geographically-stratified MASLD prevalence estimates from meta-analyses and large cohort studies (e.g., NHANES); 2) multi-level healthcare access variables (insurance coverage, PCP density, rurality index) from national databases (AHRQ, US Census); and 3) observed diagnosis rates from aggregated electronic health record data. The model will use Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to infer the posterior distribution of the undiagnosed fraction, accounting for uncertainty in all inputs. Subgroup analyses will be performed for age, T2DM, and obesity categories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first model-based, quantitative estimate of the undiagnosed MASLD burden in the US, stratified by key demographic and clinical risk factors. It will directly inform healthcare policy by identifying populations with the largest diagnostic gaps. The methodological framework can be adapted for other under-diagnosed chronic conditions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work describes diagnostic gaps qualitatively, this study applies a formal inverse problem methodology from applied mathematics to liver epidemiology, transforming prevalence data into a actionable estimate of missing diagnoses. It innovatively integrates disparate data streams (epidemiological, healthcare systems, clinical) to solve for an unknown public health variable.

proposals.title: Discovery of Protective Alleles and Microbial Signatures in Low-MASLD MENA Founder Populations: A Multi-Omic and Historical Demographic Study
proposals.motivation: Despite the global epidemic of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD), significant heterogeneity in disease prevalence exists, even among populations with high metabolic risk. Identifying the factors underlying this resilience is critical for understanding disease pathogenesis and developing targeted interventions. Populations in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region with unexpectedly low MASLD prevalence, despite high rates of obesity and type 2 diabetes, represent a unique opportunity to discover novel protective mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific MENA subpopulations with low MASLD prevalence are founder populations that harbor concentrated protective genetic variants and/or gut microbial consortia, which mitigate hepatic steatosis and fibrosis despite high metabolic risk.
proposals.method: We will first identify candidate low-MASLD subpopulations using aggregated data from multinational studies (e.g., Global NASH Council) and historical demographic analysis of migration and isolation patterns. In deeply phenotyped cohorts from these populations, we will perform whole-genome sequencing and shotgun metagenomic sequencing of stool. We will employ genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and microbial genome-wide association studies (mGWAS) to identify variants and microbial taxa/genes associated with MASLD resilience. Candidate hits will be functionally validated using in vitro hepatocyte and organoid models exposed to metabolic stressors.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify novel genetic and microbial determinants of MASLD resistance, moving beyond risk factors to discover protective biology. It will provide a foundational multi-omic dataset for understudied MENA populations. The findings could reveal new therapeutic targets aimed at mimicking natural protective mechanisms for MASLD prevention.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely applies a founder effect population genetics framework, proven in infectious disease resilience, to the complex metabolic disease MASLD. It integrates historical socio-demographic analysis with deep multi-omics to discover protection, a paradigm shift from the predominant focus on disease-associated factors in high-risk Western cohorts.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic-Validation-Informed, Stratified Meta-Analysis of Pediatric Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) Prevalence in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) Region
proposals.motivation: The MENA region has among the highest global rates of pediatric obesity and type 2 diabetes, key drivers of MASLD, yet the true burden of pediatric MASLD remains poorly characterized due to heterogeneous and often unvalidated diagnostic methods. This gap impedes accurate public health resource allocation and the development of regional screening guidelines. A prevalence estimate grounded in the local diagnostic accuracy of non-invasive tools (e.g., ultrasound, ALT, FibroScan) is urgently needed for effective clinical planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-analysis of pediatric MASLD prevalence, which first synthesizes validation data for non-invasive diagnostics in MENA and then restricts prevalence pooling to studies using the best-validated local methods, will yield a more accurate and clinically actionable prevalence estimate than traditional unstratified approaches.
proposals.method: Phase 1: A systematic review and meta-analysis of studies validating non-invasive diagnostic tools (ultrasound, serum biomarkers, transient elastography) against liver biopsy (the pre-2026 reference standard) in MENA pediatric populations. We will pool sensitivity/specificity using bivariate random-effects models and perform meta-regression by age, sex, and obesity status. Phase 2: A geographically stratified meta-analysis of pediatric MASLD prevalence studies in MENA. Inclusion will be restricted based on Phase 1 findings, prioritizing studies using tools with the highest locally validated accuracy. Prevalence will be pooled using a random-effects model, with subgroup analyses by country, age group, sex, and obesity status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first methodologically robust, diagnostic-informed estimate of pediatric MASLD burden in the MENA region, directly addressing a critical evidence gap. It will also deliver an evidence-based framework for selecting appropriate non-invasive diagnostic tools for pediatric screening in different MENA settings, guiding clinical practice and future research.
proposals.novelty: The adversarial, two-phase design explicitly uses local diagnostic validation data to filter and weight prevalence studies, moving beyond simple pooling of all available data. This ensures the final prevalence estimate is calibrated to the real-world performance of diagnostics in the target population, a refinement not previously applied to MASLD epidemiology in MENA.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of MASLD-Targeted Therapy for the Primary Prevention of De Novo Type 2 Diabetes in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: The bidirectional relationship between MASLD and type 2 diabetes (T2D) is well-established, but clinical paradigms primarily focus on managing T2D to improve liver outcomes. Given the rising global burden of both conditions and evidence that MASLD precedes and independently predicts T2D, it is critical to test whether early, targeted intervention for MASLD can alter the trajectory of dysmetabolism and prevent diabetes onset, potentially offering a more effective primary prevention strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with confirmed MASLD and significant metabolic risk factors but without established diabetes, a structured, liver-focused therapeutic intervention (combining pharmacotherapy for steatosis/fibrosis and intensive lifestyle modification) will be superior to standard of care (lifestyle advice alone) in reducing the incidence of de novo T2D over a 3-year period.
proposals.method: We propose a multinational, randomized, controlled, parallel-group trial. High-risk, non-diabetic adults (with MASLD confirmed by biopsy or MRI-PDFF, and prediabetes or high FIB-4) will be randomized 1:1 to Intervention (e.g., a GLP-1 RA with proven MASLD benefit like semaglutide, plus a structured Mediterranean diet/exercise program) or Control (standard lifestyle counseling). The primary endpoint is time to development of T2D per ADA criteria. Secondary endpoints include changes in liver fat (MRI-PDFF), liver stiffness (VCTE), and non-invasive fibrosis scores (FIB-4, NFS). Sample size will be calculated for 80% power to detect a 35% relative risk reduction. Analysis will be by intention-to-treat using Cox proportional hazards and mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a 'liver-first' prevention strategy for T2D, directly testing a causal pathway. It will define the efficacy of a combined pharmacological and lifestyle regimen in reducing diabetes incidence and improving liver histology/imaging biomarkers, informing future clinical guidelines and cost-effectiveness models for preventive care in metabolic disease.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the conventional therapeutic hierarchy by making MASLD the primary treatment target to prevent T2D, rather than viewing liver disease merely as a complication of diabetes management. It prospectively tests causality in a clinical intervention framework, moving beyond observational association.

proposals.title: Validation and Refinement of Established Cardiovascular Risk Scores for Predicting Major Adverse Cardiovascular Events in Patients with Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD)
proposals.motivation: Cardiovascular disease (CVD) is the leading cause of mortality in patients with MASLD, yet standard CVD risk calculators (e.g., ACC/AHA Pooled Cohort Equations, FRS) were developed in general populations and may not accurately capture risk in this specific, high-burden cohort. Pre-2026 evidence suggests a complex interplay between liver fibrosis, metabolic dysfunction, and CVD, necessitating a targeted risk assessment strategy to improve primary prevention and resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established CVD risk scores will demonstrate suboptimal discrimination and calibration in a MASLD population, and that the integration of MASLD-specific factors (particularly liver fibrosis severity) will significantly improve predictive accuracy for major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multinational cohort study using existing MASLD patient registries. Primary outcomes are time-to-first MACE (non-fatal MI, stroke, CVD death). We will first validate the performance (discrimination via C-statistic, calibration via Hosmer-Lemeshow) of standard scores (ASCVD, FRS). We will then develop and internally validate a refined model using Cox proportional hazards regression, incorporating standard risk factors plus MASLD-specific variables: fibrosis stage (by FIB-4, NFS, or elastography), type 2 diabetes control (HbA1c), and obesity phenotype. Model performance will be compared using net reclassification improvement (NRI).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, direct validation of common CVD risk tools in a dedicated MASLD cohort, quantifying their limitations. It will generate a pragmatic, evidence-based framework for CVD risk stratification in MASLD that integrates liver disease severity, potentially guiding more personalized surveillance and preventive therapy initiation.
proposals.novelty: While CVD risk in MASLD is acknowledged, systematic validation of existing calculators is lacking. The novelty lies in formally testing their inadequacy and proposing a clinically actionable, integrated model that moves beyond simply noting the association to providing a quantifiable prediction tool.

proposals.title: Identification of Protective Factors Against Fibrosis Progression in MASLD: A Multi-Cohort Study of Non-Progressors
proposals.motivation: Research on MASLD overwhelmingly focuses on risk factors for progression to advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis, driving a deficit in understanding why many patients remain stable. Identifying protective factors, whether genetic, metabolic, or lifestyle-based, is crucial for developing targeted prevention strategies and shifting the clinical paradigm from risk mitigation to resilience promotion. This directly addresses the high unmet need for non-pharmacologic interventions in MASLD management.
proposals.hypothesis: Individuals with MASLD who demonstrate long-term non-progression to advanced fibrosis possess distinct protective biological signatures (e.g., specific metabolomic, proteomic, or gut microbiome profiles) and modifiable lifestyle factors that mitigate hepatic injury and fibrogenesis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective multi-cohort analysis of existing longitudinal MASLD patient databases (e.g., NASH CRN, UK Biobank) to define 'non-progressors' as those with confirmed MASLD and no fibrosis progression over ≥10 years. Using pre-2026 machine learning methods, we will apply random forest and LASSO regression models to high-dimensional clinical, biochemical, and 'omics data (targeted metabolomics, proteomics via Olink, 16S rRNA sequencing) to identify features associated with non-progression. Validation will be performed in an independent prospective cohort with serial non-invasive fibrosis assessments (VCTE, FIB-4).
proposals.contribution: This study will generate the first comprehensive profile of the 'MASLD non-progressor,' identifying novel protective biomarkers and modifiable lifestyle factors. The findings will inform the development of clinical scores for predicting stable disease and lay the groundwork for interventional trials aimed at mimicking these protective mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Younossi and others has excelled in defining risk and cost of progression, this proposal uniquely inverts the framework to systematically study protection and resilience in MASLD, a largely unexplored perspective with high translational potential.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Education Platform to Improve Clinician and Patient Knowledge of MASLD Risk and Progression in High-Risk Diabetes Populations
proposals.motivation: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) is a major global health burden, with its prevalence and risk of progression to advanced fibrosis and cirrhosis being significantly higher in patients with type 2 diabetes (T2D). Despite this, awareness among both clinicians managing T2D and the patients themselves remains critically low, leading to under-screening and missed opportunities for early intervention. This gap in knowledge directly contributes to the rising incidence of liver-related morbidity and mortality, as highlighted by Dr. Younossi's prior work on the cost of hepatic decompensation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted, multi-modal digital education program, delivered to both primary care clinicians and their patients with T2D, will significantly improve knowledge of MASLD risks, increase appropriate utilization of non-invasive tests (NITs) for fibrosis, and lead to higher rates of guideline-concordant management compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a cluster-randomized controlled trial involving 40 primary care practices. The intervention arm will receive access to a bespoke digital platform containing: 1) For clinicians, accredited modules on MASLD pathophysiology, risk stratification using validated NITs (e.g., FIB-4, vibration-controlled transient elastography), and management algorithms. 2) For patients, interactive content on liver health, personalized risk feedback based on their clinical data, and shared decision-making tools. The platform will use a rules-based algorithm to tailor patient content based on individual risk factors (age, diabetes control, obesity). Primary outcomes will be changes in validated knowledge assessments and the proportion of at-risk patients receiving guideline-recommended fibrosis assessment at 6 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for the efficacy of a scalable educational intervention to bridge the knowledge-practice gap in MASLD care within diabetes management. It will yield a validated, implementable tool that can be disseminated to improve early detection of advanced fibrosis, potentially altering the disease trajectory. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines and public health strategies aimed at reducing the liver-related burden of MASLD.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have assessed knowledge gaps, this proposal innovates by simultaneously targeting the clinician-patient dyad with a digitally integrated, personalized intervention and rigorously measuring its impact on real-world clinical actions (NIT utilization) within a high-risk T2D population, moving beyond awareness to measurable practice change.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Lifestyle Behaviors for Risk Stratification and Progression Tracking in Patients with MASLD and Type 2 Diabetes: A Prospective Observational Study
proposals.motivation: The progression of Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) is driven by modifiable lifestyle factors, particularly in high-risk populations like those with type 2 diabetes (T2D). Current clinical assessments provide only episodic snapshots, failing to capture the continuous, real-world behaviors (diet, physical activity, sleep) that influence disease course. This gap limits personalized risk prediction and timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that continuous, passive digital phenotyping of daily behaviors will generate objective, granular lifestyle signatures that are more strongly associated with MASLD severity and short-term fibrosis progression risk in T2D patients than standard clinical and questionnaire-based assessments.
proposals.method: We will prospectively enroll 300 adults with T2D and biopsy-proven MASLD. Participants will use a smartphone app (e.g., Beiwe platform) and wearable accelerometer (e.g., ActiGraph) for 12 months to passively collect GPS, accelerometry, screen time, and self-reported meal logs. Digital phenotypes (activity patterns, circadian regularity, geospatial mobility) will be extracted using validated algorithms. Associations between these digital phenotypes and baseline histological severity (steatosis, fibrosis) will be analyzed using multivariate regression. For a subset with serial FibroScan at 0 and 12 months, we will employ machine learning (e.g., Random Forest or LASSO regression) to identify digital signatures predictive of fibrosis progression.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a novel framework for quantifying real-world lifestyle risk in MASLD using objective digital tools. It will identify specific behavioral phenotypes linked to advanced disease and progression, moving beyond static risk scores. The findings could inform the development of just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) for behavioral modification in chronic liver disease management.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by applying digital phenotyping methodologies, developed in psychiatry for mood and cognition, to the continuous tracking of MASLD-relevant behaviors. It moves past retrospective self-report to obtain high-frequency, objective data on the exposome, creating a dynamic risk model not previously possible in hepatology.

proposals.title: Adapting and Validating a Community Health Worker-Delivered Lifestyle Intervention for Early MASLD in High-Risk Primary Care Populations
proposals.motivation: The global burden of MASLD is immense, with most patients identified only after advanced fibrosis develops. Clinical trials demonstrate that intensive lifestyle modification can halt or reverse early disease, but these resource-heavy programs are inaccessible to most at-risk individuals in community settings. There is a critical need to translate evidence-based interventions into scalable, cost-effective models for public health and primary care to impact population-level outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, culturally adapted lifestyle intervention for early MASLD, delivered by trained community health workers within a primary care network, will be feasible, acceptable, and will lead to significant improvements in non-invasive markers of steatosis and fibrosis (e.g., FIB-4, liver stiffness measurement) compared to usual care over a 12-month period.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial in community health centers serving high-prevalence populations (obesity, type 2 diabetes). The intervention arm will receive a 12-month program co-designed with community input, featuring CHW-led group sessions on nutrition and physical activity, supported by digital tools (e.g., Fitbit, dietary logging apps) for self-monitoring. The control arm receives standard educational materials. Primary outcomes are changes in FIB-4 score and controlled attenuation parameter (CAP) from baseline to 12 months. Secondary outcomes include weight loss, adherence, and cost-effectiveness analysis using a pre-2026 Markov model framework.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, scalable blueprint for delivering early MASLD care outside tertiary hepatology clinics, directly addressing the implementation gap. It will generate critical data on the real-world efficacy and cost-effectiveness of a community-based model, informing public health policy and resource allocation for chronic liver disease prevention.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has established the efficacy of lifestyle intervention in clinical trials, this proposal innovatively focuses on the *implementation* and *adaptation* of these principles for delivery by non-specialist community health workers, integrating pragmatic trial design with cost-effectiveness analysis from a public health perspective.

It will prospectively follow a cohort stratified by age—including adolescents and young adults—to determine if early-onset MASLD follows a distinct, more aggressive progression pathway compared to later-onset disease in the same region. By analyzing how baseline risk factors (e.g., metabolic health, genetic ancestry, environmental exposures) interact with age at detection, the study aims to build predictive models for disease acceleration or stabilization. This synergy uniquely identifies modifiable targets for age-specific primary and secondary prevention before irreversible liver damage occurs.

ideas.title: Causal inference and dynamic modeling of gene-environment interactions in MASLD progression across MENA ancestries
ideas.description: This study establishes a deeply phenotyped, prospective MENA cohort to move beyond association and model causal pathways. It employs a two-step analytical core: first, using Mendelian Randomization with genetic instruments to test causal effects of modifiable lifestyle factors on MASLD incidence and severity. Second, it implements a longitudinal, time-to-event analysis framework to track how genetic risk variants interact dynamically with changing environmental exposures to drive disease progression from steatosis to metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) and fibrosis. The design explicitly tests for ancestry-specific interaction effects and causal mediation, providing a robust model for predicting personalized risk trajectories and identifying actionable intervention points.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Change Economic Impact Models to Quantify the Full Burden of MASLD in MENA
ideas.description: The original idea to quantify the healthcare costs and economic impact of MASLD in the MENA region is analogous to the challenge in climate economics of projecting the full economic burden of climate change. In that field, Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) are used to synthesize scientific data on physical impacts with economic data across sectors to estimate both direct and indirect costs under various future scenarios. Adapting this solution, we propose developing a MASLD-specific economic impact model for the MENA region. This model would systematically integrate data on disease prevalence and progression, direct medical costs (from steatosis to end-stage liver disease), and indirect socioeconomic costs (such as productivity loss and disability). By running this model under different public health and demographic scenarios, it would provide a comprehensive, multi-country, and forward-looking assessment of MASLD's total economic burden, moving beyond simple healthcare cost accounting to inform regional policy and resource allocation.

ideas.title: Calibrate and validate agent-based MASLD policy models with prospective longitudinal outcome data
ideas.description: This research fuses agent-based modeling (ABM) with longitudinal validation studies to create a dynamic, evidence-driven policy evaluation tool for MASLD. An ABM simulating population-level disease progression under various interventions will be initially parameterized using existing consensus statements and epidemiological data. Its long-term predictions for hard clinical endpoints (e.g., liver-related mortality, cardiovascular events) will then be continuously tested and refined against data from a prospective, real-world longitudinal cohort study. This iterative calibration-validation cycle will both improve the model's predictive accuracy and quantitatively assess the real-world effectiveness of consensus recommendations, enabling policymakers to simulate and compare the long-term impact of potential interventions with greater confidence.

ideas.title: Multi-Stream Bayesian Synthesis for Estimating Undiagnosed MASLD Burden
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond simplistic inverse modeling by integrating multiple, independent lines of evidence to triangulate the undiagnosed MASLD population. It employs a Bayesian synthesis model that incorporates direct, empirical signals—such as patterns of unexplained elevated liver enzymes in primary care labs, mentions of hepatic steatosis in imaging reports without a formal diagnosis code, and risk-factor prevalence in screened biobank cohorts. The model uses traditional prevalence and healthcare access data only as prior distributions, which are then updated and constrained by these diverse data streams. This approach yields a more robust and empirically grounded estimate by resolving the non-uniqueness problem inherent in the original inverse formulation.

ideas.title: Resource constraints and age-specific risk: Unmasking the true drivers of MASLD management gaps in understudied young MENA adults
ideas.description: This study investigates the primary drivers of suboptimal MASLD management in young adults (e.g., 18-35 years) in MENA regions, where prevalence remains stable or high despite lower traditional metabolic risk. It moves beyond assessing guideline confusion alone by directly testing whether systemic resource constraints (e.g., limited diagnostics, specialist access, follow-up capacity) are the dominant barrier in this understudied population. By comparing perceived management challenges among healthcare providers against actual care pathways and patient outcomes in this specific age group, the research will identify if and how drivers of poor care differ from those in older, more studied cohorts. This reveals targeted interventions—whether needing clearer guidelines or improved resource allocation—for a growing, at-risk young population.

ideas.title: Targeted mHealth Screening for High-Risk Occupational and Rural Populations to Map Disparities in MASLD Burden
ideas.description: This research direction proposes deploying adapted mobile health (mHealth) screening tools not for general population surveillance, but specifically within high-risk occupational (e.g., shift workers, industrial workers) and geographically isolated rural communities. By targeting these subgroups, the project will generate granular, real-world data on MASLD prevalence, severity, and associated risk factors that are invisible in national averages. This approach transforms mHealth from a broad screening tool into a mechanism for health equity research, directly testing the hypothesis that general estimates mask significant disparities. The collected data will provide the evidence base to advocate for and design tailored prevention and care pathways for these underserved populations.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes establishing community-based MASLD prevention pilots in high-prevalence MENA communities with a dual purpose: to implement interventions and to concurrently collect granular phenotypic, metabolic, and imaging data from participants. This community-embedded data collection directly tests the applicability of current MASLD criteria and identifies potential ethnic-specific diagnostic thresholds or risk factors. The findings from these pilots then feed back to refine the intervention strategies in real-time and generate evidence for proposing population-specific modifications to MASLD definitions, moving beyond theoretical critique to actionable, data-driven revision.

ideas.title: Dual: Identifying Protective Genetic and Microbial Alleles in Low-MASLD Populations via a Founder Effect Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from population genetics, where founder effects and protective alleles have been identified in populations resilient to diseases like HIV. The analogous solution involves deep genetic screening of unexpectedly resilient subpopulations, historical demographic analysis, and functional validation. Adapted to the MASLD context, the research will investigate MENA subpopulations with low MASLD prevalence despite high metabolic risk as potential founder populations. By conducting comprehensive multi-omics profiling and analyzing the socio-demographic and historical factors (e.g., migration patterns, traditional diets) that may have concentrated protective genetic or microbial variants, the study aims to discover novel biological mechanisms of MASLD resistance. Candidate factors will then be validated for their functional role in mitigating liver fat accumulation, offering new targets for prevention and therapy.

ideas.title: Disentangling Causal Pathways: Estimating the Direct Effect of T2D vs. Mediated Metabolic Dysfunctions on MASLD Risk in MENA
ideas.description: This study moves beyond associative prediction to quantify the causal effect of Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) on MASLD risk in MENA populations, independent of its metabolic correlates. Using a pre-specified causal diagram and longitudinal cohort data, we apply g-computation to estimate the total effect of T2D and the controlled direct effect when holding intermediate variables like dyslipidemia and hypertension constant. Sensitivity analyses rigorously test the robustness of conclusions to potential unmeasured confounding. The result is a robust, causally-informed hierarchy of metabolic drivers, clarifying whether T2D is a primary causal agent or if its predictive power is largely mediated through other dysfunctions.

ideas.title: Diagnostic-Validation-Informed, Stratified Meta-Analysis of Pediatric MASLD Prevalence in MENA
ideas.description: This adversarially improved study first systematically evaluates the accuracy and applicability of non-invasive diagnostic tools for MASLD in children and adolescents across the MENA region through a meta-analysis of validation studies. Using the findings from this diagnostic review, the second phase conducts a geographically and demographically stratified prevalence meta-analysis, restricting inclusion to studies that used the best-validated local diagnostic methods. This approach ensures the final prevalence estimates are both methodologically robust and directly interpretable for clinical and public health planning. The output provides not only a more reliable prevalence figure but also an evidence-based guideline on which diagnostic tools are most appropriate for pediatric screening in different MENA settings.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysics' Seismic Velocity Models to Calibrate MASLD Diagnostic Tools for MENA Populations
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to validate MASLD diagnostic tools against liver biopsy in MENA populations. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where generalized seismic velocity models are calibrated for new regions using dense local sensor networks, this research adapts that solution. Here, the diverse MENA population is the new 'geological province,' and the various non-invasive diagnostic scores and tools are the 'models' requiring calibration. By systematically collecting liver biopsy (the gold-standard 'dense network' data) and paired clinical data from a representative cohort, the study will validate the accuracy of existing tools (e.g., FIB-4, NFS, ELF, VCTE). The outcome will be a calibration framework—potentially involving adjusted cut-offs, a new composite index, or a decision tree—that optimizes diagnostic performance for the specific genetic, metabolic, and environmental context of the MENA region, thereby improving clinical management and research accuracy.

ideas.title: A Context-Adaptive Decision Framework for MASLD Screening in Heterogeneous T2D Populations Across the MENA Region
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a one-size-fits-all screening protocol to develop and validate a dynamic, context-adaptive decision framework for MASLD screening in T2D patients across the MENA region. The framework begins by creating a regional healthcare context map, categorizing settings by infrastructure and resource levels. It then employs a multi-criteria decision analysis model that integrates this contextual data with patient-specific clinical and genetic risk markers to generate several viable, cost-conscious screening pathways tailored to different settings. The final output is a validated implementation toolkit that allows local health systems to select and operationalize the most effective and feasible screening strategy for their specific context, thereby overcoming the critical flaw of regional heterogeneity.

In that field, solutions involved recognizing that younger patients have distinct disease biology and risk factors, leading to cohort-specific research models. Adapted here, the research would systematically investigate younger MENA adults (18-35) as a distinct cohort to identify unique genetic, metabolic, and environmental risk drivers for MASLD, moving beyond extrapolation from older, more studied groups to enable earlier, precision prevention strategies.

ideas.description: This research designs and validates a mobile health platform that moves beyond simple data collection to create an integrated screening-to-care pathway for MASLD. The system uses a proprietary algorithm to stratify users into risk tiers based on combined inputs from digital questionnaires and locally accessible biomarker tests. For high-risk individuals, the platform automatically facilitates referrals, schedules appointments, and shares data with healthcare providers. For low-to-moderate risk users, it delivers personalized, evidence-based lifestyle modification programs, thereby ensuring clinical utility and preventing system overload.

ideas.description: This research direction proposes longitudinal studies that track hard clinical endpoints in specific high-risk subgroups, such as certain occupational or rural populations, to empirically validate existing consensus-based clinical guidelines. By focusing on these subgroups, which are poorly represented by general population estimates, the research tests whether the guidelines lead to equivalent, better, or worse long-term outcomes compared to the general population. This approach directly challenges the assumption that a guideline validated in a broad population is universally applicable. The outcome is a data-driven framework for creating stratified or subgroup-specific guideline recommendations, ensuring they are effective and equitable across all segments of society.

ideas.title: Implementing a Clinical Pathway for MASLD/MASH Using Aviation Safety Checklists
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from aviation safety—the standardized operational checklist—to the challenge of implementing new global consensus recommendations for MASLD/MASH management. In aviation, checklists are used to ensure critical protocols are followed consistently, reducing error. The adapted study would design a structured, stepwise clinical implementation pathway based on the consensus guidelines, analogous to an aviation checklist. This pathway would be implemented across multiple clinical sites. The impact would be assessed using a two-pronged approach: measuring clinician adherence to the pathway (process compliance) and evaluating its effect on predefined patient outcomes, such as rates of appropriate diagnosis, staging, treatment initiation, and clinical improvement, thereby testing the real-world effectiveness of the consensus framework.

ideas.title: Adapting MASLD Criteria for MENA Populations Using Pharmacogenomic Framework
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the universal applicability of MASLD diagnostic criteria across diverse MENA ethnicities by drawing an analogy from pharmacogenomics. In pharmacogenomics, the solution to universal drug dosing failing across populations was to identify population-specific genetic polymorphisms (e.g., in drug-metabolizing enzymes like CYP450) and create adjusted guidelines. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically identify phenotypic, genetic, and metabolic markers specific to MENA subpopulations that influence MASLD presentation and progression. The outcome would be a tiered, precision diagnostic framework that modifies universal MASLD criteria with validated, population-specific components, ensuring clinical guidelines are effective and equitable for the diverse MENA phenotype.

ideas.title: Identifying Protective Trajectories: A Pediatric-to-Adult Analysis of MASLD Resilience in High-Risk MENA Populations
ideas.description: This study integrates meta-analyses of pediatric and adult MASLD prevalence across the MENA region to identify and investigate subpopulations exhibiting a discordant 'resilience phenotype': high metabolic risk coupled with unexpectedly low disease prevalence, specifically tracing this pattern from childhood into adulthood. By mapping these prevalence gaps across the life course, the research aims to distinguish between populations protected from early-onset disease versus those where onset is merely delayed. This approach will prioritize resilient cohorts for deep phenotyping to uncover genetic, epigenetic, microbial, or lifestyle factors that may confer protection during critical developmental windows. The ultimate goal is to identify modifiable protective mechanisms that could inform primary prevention strategies for at-risk youth globally.

ideas.title: Use computational modeling of disease trajectories

ideas.title: Defining and Validating Mechanistic Subtypes of MASLD in MENA Populations for Precision Diagnosis
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a general critique to empirically define distinct MASLD subtypes within Middle Eastern and North African (MENA) populations using unsupervised machine learning on deep phenotypic data. It will test the hypothesis that these data-driven subtypes exhibit significantly different relationships with the standard MASLD diagnostic metabolic criteria, identifying which specific thresholds are mismatched. Finally, by integrating genomic and proteomic data with these subtypes, the study will establish a biologically grounded framework for developing and validating ethnicity-informed diagnostic models, aiming to replace a one-size-fits-all approach with precision diagnostic pathways.

ideas.title: Applying Climate Change Cohort Modeling to Predict MASLD Trajectories in MENA
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the longitudinal cohort modeling approach from climate science to study MASLD in the MENA region. In climate science, long-term ecological research networks track environmental variables and use integrated assessment models to project future conditions under different scenarios. The adapted solution is to establish a coordinated, multi-national longitudinal cohort for MASLD in MENA with standardized data collection on clinical, metabolic, and lifestyle factors. This data would feed into predictive models that forecast the region's MASLD burden under various public health and policy scenarios (e.g., differing trajectories of urbanization, dietary shifts, and intervention programs), providing a powerful tool for proactive healthcare planning and resource allocation.

ideas.title: A Prioritization Framework for Actionable High-Risk Subgroup Identification Beyond General Population Averages
ideas.description: This research develops a standardized, quantitative framework to move from the general observation that subgroups exist to the actionable identification of which specific occupational, geographic, or behavioral subgroups constitute a priority for intervention. The system integrates multi-source data to candidate subgroups and applies a multi-criteria burden-of-risk metric, weighing factors like incidence rate ratio, outcome severity, and potential for mitigation. The output is a ranked list of subgroups that meet a defined threshold of 'actionable high risk,' providing a evidence-based tool for resource allocation and targeted public health strategy.

ideas.title: Geospatial Inverse Modeling to Map and Quantify the Undiagnosed MASLD Burden
ideas.description: This research integrates GIS mapping of MASLD prevalence with environmental correlates and inverse problem-solving techniques. It uses spatially granular data on known prevalence, environmental factors (e.g., food environment, walkability), and healthcare access metrics to create a high-resolution predictive surface of the total estimated MASLD burden. The model solves for the 'missing' diagnoses geographically, producing maps that highlight hotspots of both high underlying risk and high diagnostic failure. This enables targeted public health interventions that address both primary prevention and improved case-finding in the most vulnerable communities.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Self-Improving MENA-Wide MASLD Surveillance Network with Embedded Diagnostic Validation
ideas.description: This project establishes a large-scale, standardized MENA-wide surveillance network for MASLD, but uniquely embeds a continuous, multi-center diagnostic validation study as its core operational engine. The network will systematically collect parallel data from imaging, blood-based scores, and liver biopsies across diverse sub-populations, using this real-world data to iteratively validate and adjust diagnostic algorithms. This synergy enables the creation of regionally optimized, evidence-based diagnostic pathways that are dynamically updated, ensuring the surveillance data itself becomes increasingly accurate and representative over time. The result is a sustainable infrastructure that simultaneously monitors disease burden and advances diagnostic precision in a continuous feedback loop.

ideas.title: Scale down to city or community-level NAFLD surveillance

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal Geospatial Analysis of MASLD Progression in the MENA Region
ideas.description: This research integrates longitudinal cohort data with advanced geospatial mapping to create dynamic, time-series maps of MASLD incidence, progression, and regression across the MENA region. By repeatedly geocoding participants' locations and health data over time, the study will correlate shifts in individual disease severity with temporal changes in localized environmental factors, such as air quality, urbanization, and food environment access. This approach moves beyond static hotspot identification to model how populations within specific geographic clusters evolve, identifying areas where disease progression accelerates. The resulting spatiotemporal models can pinpoint high-risk trajectories and test the causal influence of modifiable place-based factors on MASLD outcomes.

ideas.title: Network Meta-Analysis of City-Level MASLD Drivers to Rank Context-Specific Intervention Priorities
ideas.description: This research applies network meta-analysis (NMA) techniques, traditionally used for comparing treatments, to the problem of conflicting and aggregated public health guidance. Instead of analyzing drug trials, the 'treatments' in the network are the diverse socio-economic, environmental, and clinical drivers of MASLD identified through hyper-local, city-level epidemiological studies. By constructing a network of evidence from multiple cities, the analysis can quantitatively rank which drivers (e.g., specific food environments, occupational patterns, genetic admixtures) have the strongest inferred association with disease burden in different urban contexts. This yields a data-driven, prioritized list of intervention targets for each city, synthesizing localized evidence into actionable, ranked recommendations that are directly comparable across different urban settings.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference to transplant etiology trends
ideas.description: Use methods from economics to identify causal drivers of the MASH and ALD increases.

ideas.title: Hyper-local pharmaco-epidemiology for stratified MASH therapy implementation
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to invert the traditional top-down consensus model by building a city-level evidence base that integrates hyper-local MASLD/MASH driver profiles (e.g., dietary patterns, environmental exposures, genetic admixture, healthcare access) with the pharmacodynamic and real-world performance data of newly approved pharmacotherapies. The goal is to generate predictive, micro-geographic maps of which drug or drug combination is likely to achieve the highest population-level benefit and adherence in specific urban contexts, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all treatment algorithm. This enables the creation of a living, location-stratified implementation playbook that guides policymakers and health systems on formulary decisions and care pathway design tailored to their unique population needs as the therapeutic landscape evolves.

ideas.title: A validated, uncertainty-aware multi-model system for robust MASLD policy evaluation
ideas.description: This research creates a robust modeling platform to evaluate public health interventions for MASLD by pairing an agent-based model with a system dynamics model for cross-validation. It rigorously grounds model parameters using historical epidemiological data, randomized controlled trial results, and pilot intervention studies to calibrate individual behavior and disease progression. The system then performs exhaustive global sensitivity and uncertainty analyses to identify which inputs and structural assumptions most influence policy outcomes. Finally, it presents decision-makers with policy impact projections accompanied by explicit confidence intervals and scenario analyses, highlighting robust findings versus those sensitive to uncertain assumptions.

ideas.title: Context-Adaptive, Multi-Site MASLD Prevention Trials for Generalizable Community Implementation
ideas.description: This research implements a coordinated network of MASLD prevention programs across a strategically selected portfolio of high-prevalence communities with diverse socioeconomic, cultural, and infrastructural characteristics. The core intervention is standardized, but delivery mechanisms and support structures are intentionally varied. The system is designed to disentangle the intervention's efficacy from local contextual factors by continuously collecting data on implementation barriers, costs, and community engagement. The primary outcome is not just health improvement in pilot sites, but the generation of a validated, context-sensitive implementation framework that predicts success factors and required adaptations for scaling in any new community.

ideas.title: Expand consensus to pediatric MASLD and special populations
ideas.description: Apply a similar consensus-building methodology to guidelines for pediatric MASLD and other special groups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Global Consensus Recommendations for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease and Steatohepatitis.

ideas.title: Community-Level MASLD Intervention Using Ecological Restoration Principles
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original community-level MASLD intervention idea through an analogy to ecological restoration. In restoration ecology, degraded local ecosystems are rehabilitated through a systematic process: baseline assessment of key drivers, followed by simultaneous, targeted interventions on multiple fronts (e.g., reintroducing keystone species, improving soil and water conditions) to push the system toward a resilient, healthy state. Adapted to MASLD prevention, the research would implement and evaluate a pilot in a high-prevalence community by first conducting a deep assessment of the 'metabolic habitat'—mapping food access, physical activity opportunities, and social determinants. The intervention itself would be a multi-component 'restoration' strategy, co-designed with the community, targeting several key leverage points at once (e.g., partnering with local stores to increase healthy food availability, creating safe walking routes, and establishing peer support groups). The evaluation would monitor both individual metabolic biomarkers and community-level indicators over time, using an adaptive management approach to refine tactics, with the goal of creating a self-sustaining shift toward a community environment that inherently supports metabolic health and prevents MASLD.

ideas.title: A Framework-Driven Microsimulation to Quantify and Qualify the Economic Burden of MASLD Across Heterogeneous MENA Health Systems
ideas.description: This study first develops and validates a MENA-specific framework that categorizes countries by data readiness and health financing structure. Using this framework, it implements a transparent microsimulation model to project the direct and indirect costs of MASLD progression. The model is calibrated using available regional data, with gaps filled via validated imputation techniques and expert elicitation, producing tiered estimates with explicit confidence intervals. The final output is a comparable regional economic burden assessment, accompanied by a qualitative policy map linking the reliability of each country's estimate to actionable data infrastructure investments.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework Integrating Individual-Level Longitudinal Data with Geospatial Exposure Assessment for MASLD Etiology
ideas.description: This study establishes a longitudinal cohort with deep phenotyping for MASLD and links each participant to a dynamic, high-resolution geospatial exposure model capturing environmental factors like PM2.5, nitrogen dioxide, access to green space, and food environment quality over time. Using a causal inference approach with advanced statistical methods to control for individual-level confounders and behaviors, the system tests specific hypotheses about environmental contributions to MASLD incidence and progression. This design directly addresses the ecological fallacy by estimating the effect of environmental exposures at the individual level, transforming a descriptive hotspot map into an analytical tool for identifying modifiable risk factors.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Cancer treatments affecting MASLD
ideas.description: Study how oncologic therapies influence the progression or regression of concurrent MASLD.
