ideas.title: Adapting Citizen Science Bioacoustics Models for mHealth-Based Liver Sound Screening
ideas.description: This idea expands the original mHealth screening concept by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically large-scale biodiversity monitoring via citizen science bioacoustics. In that field, smartphone apps collect environmental audio from non-experts, and cloud-based AI models analyze the clips to identify species. We adapt this solution to liver screening by developing an mHealth application that guides users to capture abdominal sounds. The audio data is analyzed by a specialized AI model to detect acoustic biomarkers associated with MASLD, enabling low-cost, population-scale screening and data aggregation without the need for centralized clinical visits for initial risk stratification.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Change Intervention Modeling to Simulate MASLD Policy Scenarios
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution approach from climate science's Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) to the context of MASLD policy. In climate policy, IAMs are used to simulate the long-term environmental and economic outcomes of different carbon mitigation strategies under deep uncertainty. The adapted idea creates a similar multi-scale modeling framework for MASLD. It combines an agent-based model simulating individual-level disease progression and behavioral responses with system dynamics models capturing population-level healthcare resource flows and policy implementation dynamics. This integrated model would allow policymakers to simulate and compare the long-term health outcomes, healthcare costs, and equity impacts of different public health intervention bundles—such as combined dietary guidelines, screening programs, and treatment access policies—under various socioeconomic and biological uncertainty scenarios, mirroring how climate IAMs evaluate policy pathways.

ideas.title: Adapting the Global Seismographic Network model for a MENA-wide MASLD surveillance network
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics. The analogous problem is monitoring a diffuse, widespread, and dynamically changing phenomenon (seismic activity) across a large, heterogeneous geographic region, which was solved by the Global Seismographic Network (GSN) through standardized sensors, real-time data transmission, and centralized processing. Adapted to the MENA MASLD context, the solution is to create a regional surveillance network modeled on the GSN. This involves deploying standardized diagnostic and data-collection 'sensors' at sentinel sites across the region, using common protocols for screening and staging. Data would flow to a central coordinating hub that harmonizes information, applies consistent analytical models, and disseminates findings, thereby scaling a fragmented, national-level challenge into a unified, ongoing regional surveillance system for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD).

ideas.title: Validate consensus statements with long-term outcome data
ideas.description: Conduct longitudinal studies to validate the consensus recommendations against hard clinical endpoints.
ideas.sourceTitle: Global Consensus Recommendations for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease and Steatohepatitis.

ideas.title: Transfer microsimulation from health economics to MASLD
ideas.description: Use microsimulation modeling, common in health economics, to project individual-level MASLD progression and costs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Epidemiology of metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease

ideas.title: Participatory Framework for Context-Specific MASLD Guidelines in Special Populations
ideas.description: This research system establishes a participatory, multi-stakeholder framework to develop context-specific MASLD guidelines for pediatric and other special populations. It begins with in-depth qualitative evidence synthesis and targeted engagement of patients, caregivers, and frontline providers from diverse settings to identify population-specific priorities, barriers, and equity gaps. These foundational insights are used to co-create adaptable guideline modules and core principles, which then serve as the mandatory input for a subsequent modified Delphi or formal consensus process. This ensures the final consensus guidelines are not only evidence-based but are also pragmatically designed for implementation and equity across heterogeneous care environments.

ideas.title: A Living, Prospectively-Validated Consensus Framework Using Real-World Data and Predictive Analytics
ideas.description: This system transforms consensus validation from a passive, long-term audit into an active, integrated component of guideline development and maintenance. It mandates the creation of a prospective registry alongside the publication of any new consensus statement, collecting high-frequency real-world data on surrogate endpoints, treatment adherence, and intermediate outcomes. Using advanced modeling techniques, this data is continuously analyzed to predict long-term clinical endpoints and estimate the real-world effectiveness of the recommendations. The consensus panel receives periodic predictive validation reports, enabling a dynamic, learning system where guidelines can be iteratively refined based on early signals of benefit or harm, dramatically reducing the evidence-update cycle.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: derive guidelines from outcomes data
ideas.description: Invert the process by mining real-world outcomes data to infer effective care components.
ideas.sourceTitle: 4. Comprehensive Medical Evaluation and Assessment of Comorbidities: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2025.

ideas.title: Applying Microbiome Mapping to Unmask City-Specific MASLD Drivers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that national or regional data aggregation is sufficient for planning MASLD interventions by drawing an analogy from microbial ecology. In microbiome research, aggregating data from broad body sites masked how local microenvironments (e.g., gut lumen vs. mucosa) create distinct, functionally critical microbial communities. The analogous solution was a shift to high-resolution, site-specific mapping and analysis. Adapted to MASLD, this means inverting the regional epidemiological approach to prioritize hyper-local, city- or even neighborhood-level mapping of disease prevalence, metabolic drivers, and socio-environmental determinants. By treating each urban area's unique characteristics as a defining 'microenvironment,' this approach aims to uncover the specific dietary, economic, and built-environment factors driving MASLD in that locale, thereby revealing precise, targeted intervention needs that country-level data inherently masks.

ideas.title: Apply multi-criteria decision analysis for guideline prioritization
ideas.description: Use MCDA to weight and prioritize consensus statements based on efficacy, cost, and feasibility.
ideas.sourceTitle: Global Consensus Recommendations for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease and Steatohepatitis.

ideas.title: Ontology-Driven Multi-Criteria Framework for Deconstructing and Contextualizing Guideline Recommendations
ideas.description: This system first formally characterizes each guideline recommendation using a structured ontology that captures its evidence base, development process, strength, and clinical context. These ontological profiles are then analyzed within a multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA) framework, where stakeholders can weight the importance of different criteria (e.g., evidence quality vs. patient preferences) for a specific clinical scenario. The output is not a single ranked list, but a set of contextualized, scenario-specific syntheses that make the reasoning behind conflicting recommendations transparent and actionable for decision-makers.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Identify Primary Constraints in MASLD Management
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that guideline confusion is the primary driver of suboptimal MASLD management by proposing an analogical approach from community ecology. In ecology, the 'limiting factor' concept is used to identify the single most critical resource constraining a species' growth, rather than assuming all environmental factors are equally important. The analogous solution involves systematically manipulating or measuring multiple potential constraints to see which yields the largest effect on the outcome. Adapted to this context, the research would design a study to test and rank the relative impact of guideline confusion against other candidate constraints—such as time pressures, resource limitations, diagnostic access, or financial disincentives—on the quality of MASLD management in primary care. The adapted idea is to move beyond listing barriers and instead identify the primary 'limiting factor(s)' that, if alleviated, would produce the greatest improvement in care, thereby offering a more targeted and efficient strategy for health system intervention.

ideas.title: A Fairness-Constrained Active Learning System for Equitable MASLD Risk Prediction in the MENA Region
ideas.description: This research designs an ML system that proactively identifies and mitigates bias in MASLD prediction for MENA populations. It begins with a comprehensive bias audit of existing clinical data to map diagnostic gaps. The core of the approach is an active learning pipeline that iteratively queries for new data samples from the most underrepresented subgroups, thereby improving both model performance and fairness. The final predictive model is trained with adversarial debiasing techniques, explicitly optimizing for clinical accuracy while constraining for equitable performance across gender, geographic, and socioeconomic strata.

ideas.title: Data aggregation masks country-specific intervention needs

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map MASLD Risk Landscapes in MENA Populations
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Conservation Biology, where Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) integrates species data with environmental layers to predict distributions. The analogous problem is mapping a disease 'niche' instead of a species niche. The adapted idea is to investigate MASLD drivers in MENA subpopulations by constructing a multi-layered risk model. Genetic data (e.g., PNPLA3, TM6SF2 variants common in the region) and detailed lifestyle/exposure data (dietary composition, activity levels, socioeconomic factors) would serve as the 'environmental layers.' By integrating these with MASLD prevalence data across different subpopulations, the model would identify the key interacting drivers (GxE) and generate a predictive 'risk landscape' map, highlighting geographic and demographic clusters most vulnerable to MASLD under current and projected lifestyle trends.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Real-Time MASLD Surveillance Network for the MENA Region
ideas.description: This research establishes a MENA-wide MASLD surveillance network designed not just to collect standardized data, but to actively generate and test region-specific adaptation protocols. The network will use its ongoing data stream to identify local variations in disease presentation, resource availability, and care pathways, iteratively developing and validating tailored diagnostic algorithms, referral criteria, and management frameworks. This creates a closed-loop system where surveillance directly informs and refines practical, locally executable consensus guidelines. Consequently, the network evolves from a passive monitoring tool into an active engine for generating context-specific, evidence-based public health strategies for MASLD across diverse MENA health systems.

ideas.title: Dark Matter Mapping: Estimating Undiagnosed MASLD Burden via Healthcare Access and Epidemiological Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cosmological solution for estimating dark matter to the public health inverse problem of estimating undiagnosed MASLD cases. In cosmology, the mass and distribution of unseen dark matter are inferred from its effects on visible structures and light. Analogously, we propose to treat diagnosed MASLD cases as the visible structure and use detailed data on healthcare access (e.g., primary care density, insurance coverage, specialist availability, historical screening patterns) as the forces that 'bend' the path to diagnosis. By building a spatial or demographic model that relates observed prevalence to these system-level 'gravitational' parameters, we can run an inverse estimation to back-calculate the likely total (diagnosed + undiagnosed) disease burden in different populations. The output would be a quantified map of missing diagnoses, revealing the 'dark matter' of MASLD epidemiology.

ideas.title: Modeling the System: Decomposing and Mapping the Interacting Drivers of MASLD Management Gaps
ideas.description: This study moves beyond identifying a primary driver to model the healthcare system delivering MASLD care. It employs a quantitative component to decompose the relative weight and interactions of clinician knowledge, perceived barriers, resource availability, and practice environment. Concurrently, a qualitative component engages clinicians and administrators in constructing causal loop diagrams to reveal systemic feedback mechanisms, such as how low referral access diminishes diagnostic confidence. The integrated analysis produces a testable system model that identifies high-leverage intervention points, not just dominant constraints.

ideas.title: Develop a dynamic, stratified consensus framework for pharmacotherapy in pediatric and special population MASLD/MASH
ideas.description: This research will develop a consensus methodology specifically designed to generate and continuously update evidence-based, stratified treatment algorithms for pediatric MASLD/MASH and other special populations (e.g., post-transplant, lean MASLD). The core innovation is a structured framework that integrates real-world data and anticipated clinical trial results from the post-resmetirom drug pipeline to create population-specific recommendations on drug eligibility, sequencing, and combination therapy. This addresses the critical gap where new adult-focused therapies are approved without clear guidance for use in children or complex sub-groups, ensuring these populations benefit equitably from therapeutic advances within a rigorous, ethically sound consensus structure.

ideas.title: Adapt Pediatric Oncology Precision Medicine Framework for Pediatric MASLD Consensus
ideas.description: This idea proposes using an analogical solution from pediatric oncology to expand consensus to pediatric MASLD and special populations. In pediatric oncology, consensus for complex childhood cancers is built using a dedicated precision medicine framework that integrates developmental biology, distinct pediatric pathophysiology, and lifespan outcomes, rather than extrapolating from adult guidelines. Adapting this solution, a dedicated Pediatric & Special Populations MASLD Task Force would be established to create consensus guidelines. This task force would systematically integrate core elements like developmental hepatology, puberty-specific metabolic changes, pediatric-specific diagnostic criteria (e.g., ALT), pharmacokinetics in children, and long-term health outcome metrics relevant to a 70+ year lifespan, ensuring the guidelines are fundamentally designed for these unique populations from the outset.

ideas.title: City-Level Meta-Regression to Decode Geographic Heterogeneity in MASLD Drivers
ideas.description: This research direction applies advanced, multi-level meta-regression models to a systematically assembled global dataset of city- or sub-national MASLD epidemiology studies. Instead of treating country or region as a crude covariate, the model explicitly incorporates high-resolution, city-specific data on drivers like food environments, physical activity infrastructure, air pollution, and economic indices. This allows the decomposition of heterogeneity in pooled estimates into quantifiable shares explained by these localized contextual factors, moving beyond identifying that variance exists to precisely explaining why it exists in specific urban contexts. The output is a transferable model that can predict intervention impact based on a city's unique driver profile, directly informing hyper-local policy needs.

ideas.title: Adaptive Sentinel Network with Hybrid Follow-up for MASLD Progression in MENA
ideas.description: This study establishes a decentralized network of clinical sentinel sites across diverse MENA sub-regions to track MASLD progression. It employs a hybrid follow-up model, combining core biennial clinical assessments with continuous remote monitoring via validated digital tools to maintain participant engagement and reduce attrition. The design incorporates adaptive, rolling recruitment at sentinel sites to replenish cohorts and uses advanced statistical methods to explicitly model and correct for loss to follow-up. This creates a robust, real-world evidence system capable of generating reliable longitudinal data on disease progression despite regional challenges in long-term cohort stability.

ideas.title: Causal Meta-Analysis: Integrating Directed Acyclic Graphs and Sensitivity Analysis to Explain Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond correlational meta-regression by embedding causal inference principles into the meta-analytic workflow. After initial exploratory meta-regression, researchers explicitly define a causal model (using Directed Acyclic Graphs) of how study characteristics are hypothesized to influence prevalence estimates. The system then employs bias-analysis techniques, such as quantitative bias modeling and E-value sensitivity analysis, to assess the robustness of these associations to unmeasured confounding and selection bias. The final output is a causally-informed assessment of heterogeneity sources, distinguishing plausible mechanistic drivers from spurious correlates.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map High-Risk Occupational Health Subgroups
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that general population prevalence adequately captures risk in specific occupational or rural groups by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, Species Distribution Models solve the analogous problem of assuming a species is evenly distributed across a region by instead modeling how specific environmental variables define a suitable 'niche,' revealing concentrated areas of high probability. The adapted research idea applies this niche modeling framework to public health. By treating high-risk occupational subgroups analogously to a species, and replacing environmental variables with job-specific, locational, and behavioral risk factors, researchers can build models to identify and map the specific 'risk niches' where prevalence is concentrated. This moves beyond broad averages to proactively target subgroups, such as specific trades in certain rural areas, for surveillance and intervention.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian ranking models from ecology to synthesize guideline recommendations
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from community ecology, where Bayesian hierarchical ranking models (like Bradley-Terry models) are used to infer latent species dominance hierarchies from incomplete and conflicting pairwise interaction data. The analogous problem in guideline synthesis is the presence of multiple, often conflicting, recommendations from different guideline bodies on overlapping clinical topics, with no direct, comprehensive comparison. The adapted research idea applies this Bayesian ranking framework to quantitatively synthesize guideline recommendations. Guideline statements on specific clinical questions are treated as observed 'interaction outcomes.' The model infers a latent reliability or consensus score for each guideline-developing organization by analyzing the network of agreements and disagreements across the corpus of guidelines, ultimately producing a probabilistic ranking that quantitatively resolves conflicts and highlights the most consistently reliable sources.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Risk Assessment Methods for MASLD Risk Prediction in MENA
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geophysics, where machine learning integrates heterogeneous data to map regional seismic hazard, to the problem of MASLD risk prediction in the MENA region. The analogous solution uses ML on historical and geological data to create probabilistic risk maps for earthquakes. Adapted here, we propose applying similar ensemble ML algorithms not only to demographic and clinical data for individual MASLD prediction but to synthesize this data spatially. The goal is to generate population-level, geographic risk heatmaps for MASLD across MENA countries. This identifies high-burden clusters and visualizes the complex interplay of metabolic, demographic, and potentially environmental 'fault lines', providing a powerful public health tool for targeted intervention, mirroring how seismic maps guide infrastructure reinforcement.

ideas.title: Adapting Global Healthcare Consensus via Ecological Niche Modeling Frameworks
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Conservation Biology, this idea adapts the solution of ecological niche modeling to the challenge of creating region-specific healthcare adaptation protocols. In ecology, global species distribution models are downscaled to local contexts by integrating site-specific variables. Similarly, this research proposes to develop a formal methodological framework where a global healthcare consensus (e.g., for disease treatment or public health intervention) is systematically analyzed and adapted by integrating local 'variables' such as workforce capacity, equipment availability, cultural acceptance, and financial resources. The goal is to create a reproducible, transparent toolkit that guides stakeholders in translating high-level global agreements into actionable, optimized, and sustainable local healthcare protocols, thereby bridging the gap between international standards and on-the-ground reality.

ideas.title: Applying Stratigraphic Analysis to Guideline Evolution
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from geology/stratigraphy, where the complete historical sequence of rock layers is analyzed to understand Earth's history, to the research context of clinical guidelines. The analogous solution is to treat the exclusion of older 'strata' (pre-2018 guidelines) as an artificial constraint that obscures historical context. The adapted research idea is to relax the timeframe assumption by applying a stratigraphic analysis framework: systematically include the full sequence of guideline versions. This allows researchers to map the 'knowledge deposition' over time, identify 'unconformities' or radical shifts in recommendations, and trace the lineage of current disagreements back to their origins in earlier evidence interpretations or societal values, thereby providing a deeper, more contextual understanding of present-day medical discourse.

ideas.title: Stratified Historical Epistemology for Guideline Evolution Analysis
ideas.description: This research systematically evaluates the value of historical clinical guidelines by applying a structured framework of historical epistemology rather than a simple temporal inclusion. It first classifies pre-2018 documents along dimensions of epistemic status and influence to filter out obsolete or irrelevant material. The core analysis then traces the specific conceptual and evidentiary lineages of points of current disagreement, identifying which historical positions, evidence, or rationales are genuine precursors versus abandoned paths. This method ensures that included historical context directly and accurately illuminates the roots and evolution of present-day controversies, avoiding anachronism and narrative oversimplification.

ideas.title: A Federated, Incentive-Aligned Framework for Adaptive MASLD Surveillance Across Heterogeneous MENA Health Systems
ideas.description: This initiative establishes a lightweight, federated network for MASLD monitoring that accommodates the region's extreme heterogeneity. It operates on a hub-and-spoke model where a central coordinating body defines only a minimal, essential dataset, while individual national nodes retain full control over data collection methods tailored to their local capabilities and regulations. The system is sustained by a formal compact offering technical support for capacity building, data sovereignty guarantees, and shared analytics benefits to all participating nations. This approach ensures long-term viability and generates comparable regional insights without requiring uniform infrastructure or legal harmonization.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Versioned Consensus Framework for MASH Pharmacotherapy
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of incorporating future MASH drug approvals into a global consensus by drawing an analogy from software engineering's approach to managing evolving codebases. The analogous solution uses version control and package management to systematically integrate updates. Adapted to MASH, this means developing a formal, versioned consensus framework that operates like a living, managed repository. Upon regulatory approval of a new pharmacotherapy (e.g., post-resmetirom), a predefined protocol is activated to integrate the new agent's data, reassess its role relative to existing therapies and patient strata (akin to managing dependencies), and issue an updated, versioned consensus statement. This creates a structured, transparent, and timely mechanism for evolving clinical guidance, moving beyond sporadic, ad-hoc consensus conferences to a dynamic, managed process.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Theory to Standardization: A Framework for Competing Guidelines
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the need for a single global consensus by drawing an analogy from ecology. In ecology, niche theory demonstrates how biodiversity and ecosystem resilience arise from multiple species occupying distinct roles, not from a single dominant species. The analogous solution involves resource partitioning and character displacement, allowing coexistence and system-wide health. Adapted to the context of technical or regulatory standards, this research would investigate if deliberately fostering competing, context-specific guidelines—each occupying a distinct 'innovation niche' defined by factors like regional needs, industry sector, or development stage—drives more innovation and adaptive capacity than a single harmonized global standard. The study would develop a framework to analyze how such 'standard niches' partition the problem space, interact, and collectively contribute to the health of the technological or regulatory ecosystem.

ideas.description: This system begins by formally capturing the local healthcare ecosystem's irreducible constraints, including resource ceilings, cultural norms, and existing practitioner workflows, into a structured 'Local Reality Specification'. This specification then acts as the mandatory foundation and filter for all subsequent design, forcing a critical evaluation of which elements of a global consensus can be genuinely integrated, modified, or must be replaced by novel, locally-generated solutions. The output is not an adapted global framework but a bespoke protocol engineered from the ground up to be feasible, legitimate, and sustainable within its specific operating environment, with traceability back to both local inputs and global evidence.

ideas.description: This redesigned Delphi protocol maintains a strict supermajority requirement (e.g., 70-80%) for defining the primary consensus recommendation to preserve methodological integrity. In parallel, it implements a formal, quantitative tracking system across rounds that identifies and documents persistent minority opinion clusters, measuring their stability and reasoning. The final output explicitly reports both the supermajority consensus and any well-defined, stable minority viewpoints, providing a complete picture of expert opinion that highlights emerging ideas and nuanced dissent without compromising the decisiveness of the core consensus.

ideas.title: Apply Geostatistical Kriging to Meta-Regression for Spatial Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geostatistics to address heterogeneity in meta-analysis. In geology, kriging uses variogram models to dissect spatial variance into structured autocorrelation and random noise for optimal prediction. Applying this to meta-regression, we would treat study characteristics (e.g., latitude/longitude, climatic variables) as spatial coordinates and model the residuals from a standard meta-regression to construct a variogram. This allows us to explicitly partition the total heterogeneity (I² or Tau²) into a component explained by the spatial autocorrelation structure of the studies and a remaining pure sampling/random component, offering a more nuanced dissection of variance sources in prevalence estimates, particularly for geographically influenced health outcomes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Thresholding for Delphi Consensus Using Signal Detection Theory
ideas.description: This research seed adapts the concept of adaptive thresholding from signal processing and neuroscience to challenge the assumption that a fixed supermajority is optimal for Delphi studies. In neural spike detection, adaptive thresholds are used to capture both strong and weak-but-informative signals amidst noise. The analogous solution involves dynamically adjusting the detection criterion based on signal-to-noise ratios. Adapted to the Delphi context, this means replacing the fixed supermajority requirement for accepting a recommendation with a dynamic agreement threshold. This threshold would be algorithmically adjusted per round, considering factors like the variance in responses, the convergence trend, and the entropy of the rating distribution. The core test is whether this adaptive system better captures important nuanced or emerging expert recommendations that a rigid high bar might prematurely exclude, potentially leading to richer, more responsive consensus findings.

ideas.title: Optimizing Innovation through Tiered Governance: A Minimal Global Core with Context-Specific Sandboxes
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid governance model to overcome the pitfalls of pure fragmentation or rigid harmonization. It posits that a minimal, foundational global consensus on critical aspects like interoperability, safety, and ethical baselines is necessary to prevent systemic failure and enable collaboration. The core question shifts to defining this minimal core and designing mechanisms—such as regulatory sandboxes, mutual recognition agreements, and standardized innovation metrics—that allow competing, context-specific guidelines to flourish safely within its framework. The outcome is a principled framework for dynamically managing the tension between local innovation and global coordination.
